roots.title: Data Visualization Techniques and Social Media Trends
roots.summary: The segment introduces data visualization techniques and tools essential for data science, particularly after exploratory data analysis (EDA). It emphasizes coding for creating plots using Python libraries and the importance of visualizing distributions and kernel density estimation. Key techniques such as line plots, scatter plots, and histograms are discussed with practical examples, including birth weight distribution and movie data analysis. Effective visualization focuses on clarity, accessibility, and context for communicating results to stakeholders. Additionally, a survey on U.S. teens' social media trends reveals sharing accomplishments, family topics, and emotional discussions, while highlighting the validity of distributions in visualizations. Bar plots are identified as effective for qualitative variables and small counts of discrete quantitative variables, with lengths indicating proportions. The segment also mentions using the World Bank dataset for visualizing trends with Matplotlib and Seaborn.

children.title: Introduction to Data Visualization Techniques and Tools
children.summary: The segment covers visualization techniques in data science, emphasizing their importance after exploratory data analysis (EDA). It discusses necessary coding for creating plots using Python libraries and highlights the significance of visualizing distributions and kernel density estimation. Key techniques such as line plots, scatter plots, and histograms are introduced, with practical examples including birth weight distribution and movie data analysis. Effective visualization is framed around clarity, accessibility, and context, focusing on the communication of results to stakeholders.

children.title: Introduction to Data Visualization Techniques and Tools
children.summary: The lecture introduces visualization techniques in data science, highlighting its popularity among students and its critical role in the data science life cycle, particularly after exploratory data analysis (EDA). It discusses necessary coding for creating various plots using relevant Python libraries and emphasizes the importance of visualizing distributions and kernel density estimation. Key visualization techniques such as line plots, scatter plots, and histograms will be covered, with practical examples like birth weight distribution and movie data analysis. Effective data visualization focuses on clarity, accessibility, and context, prioritizing understanding and communication of results to stakeholders.

children.title: Introduction to Visualization Techniques in Data 100
children.summary: The lecture introduces visualization techniques, emphasizing its popularity among students as indicated by survey feedback. The topic will be discussed in this session and will continue in the next class.

children.title: Introduction to Visualization in Data 100
children.summary: The lecture focuses on visualization techniques, highlighting its popularity among students based on survey feedback. The topic will be covered in this session and continued in the next class.

children.title: Introduction to Visualization Techniques and Tools
children.summary: The lecture introduces visualization techniques and tools, emphasizing the importance of data visualization in the data science life cycle, particularly after exploratory data analysis. It covers the types of visualizations needed, the coding necessary to create various plots, and relevant Python libraries and their interrelationships. Additionally, it highlights the significance of visualizing distributions and kernel density estimation, building on previous topics such as data manipulation, imputation, and string functions.

children.title: Introduction to Visualization Techniques and Tools
children.summary: The lecture will cover the importance of data visualization, the types of visualizations needed, and the coding necessary to create various plots. It will also introduce relevant Python libraries and their interrelationships, aiming to equip students with the skills to generate their own visualizations.

leaf_chunk.text:

children.title: Importance and Goals of Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of visualization in the data science life cycle, emphasizing its role after exploratory data analysis. It highlights previous topics such as data manipulation, imputation, and string functions, and sets the agenda for creating visualizations following data cleaning. Key concepts include visualizing distributions and kernel density estimation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So first off, I'm going to start by talking about why do we care about visualization and what is the goal of visualization. So this is kind of the data science life cycle that by now you're all familiar with. We have spent a lot of time in exploratory data analysis. We've seen how we can manipulate the data, how can we do an imputation, how can we make sense of whether our data is in the right format or not. We've seen a lot of the string functions, right? So last lecture was a lot of the string functions and regular expressions. What we're going to do today is actually make a visualization of our data set after we do a little bit of data cleaning. children.title: Role and Techniques of Data Visualization
children.summary: Visualization is vital in the data science lifecycle, especially during exploratory data analysis (EDA) and reporting insights. Upcoming weeks will focus on prediction and inference modeling, with key areas including visualization principles and tools like Seaborn. Various visualization techniques such as line plots, scatter plots, and histograms will be emphasized, with practical examples like birth weight distribution and movie data analysis. The goals of data visualization are to enhance understanding during EDA and communicate results effectively to stakeholders, focusing on clarity, accessibility, and context in visualizations.

children.title: Visualization in Data Science Lifecycle
children.summary: Visualization plays a crucial role in the data science lifecycle, particularly during the exploratory data analysis (EDA) phase and when reporting insights to stakeholders. The upcoming weeks will focus on prediction and inference modeling, emphasizing the importance of preparing data for these phases. Key areas of study include visualization principles, working with text data, and using tools like Seaborn for creating effective plots.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we'll make visualization so that we summarize the trends and then we basically try to understand whether our data is ready for the next phase of the data science lifecycle, life cycle which is going to be the prediction and inference that we're going to go into pretty quickly in a couple of weeks we're going to see a lot of modeling. in data 100. So the places that we will do visualization in a data science life cycle, as you can see, is in two places. I also kind of bolded the report decision and solutions. So either we make visualization in the EDA phase or we make visualization in the part that we'll make reports or we're trying to share our insights when stakeholders are outsiders or outsiders of the data science life cycle. children.title: Introduction to Visualization Techniques
children.summary: The segment discusses various visualization techniques used in Data 8 and Data 100, including line plots, scatter plots, and histograms. It emphasizes the importance of effective visualization and introduces the programming library that will be utilized for creating these visualizations. Additionally, it mentions specific examples such as plotting the distribution of birth weight and analyzing movie data over the years.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You have also seen visualization before so it's not going to be entirely new to how we make visualization in different plots. It's just going to be a different programming library that you will learn and we're going to also talk a little bit about the science behind making visualization and how to make a very effective visualization in data 100. You've seen line plot in data 8, those coming from data 8, you've seen a scatter plot and also homework 1, you had also plotted the histograms and data tends to really like histograms. And we're going to talk a little bit about histograms and how we can applaud it using the tools that you have in data 100. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Goals of Data Visualization in Analysis
children.summary: The primary goals of data visualization are to enhance understanding of data during exploratory data analysis and to effectively communicate results to stakeholders. Visualization aids in making sense of data by allowing quick and flexible adjustments to display trends across different columns. Emphasis is placed on creating lightweight visuals that provide a summary of specific data sets, with a focus on descriptive techniques to be discussed in the next lecture.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the goal of general visualization, kind of going back to the data style cycle that I showed you is to kind of provide a high level description of the data or a high level visual of the data to ourselves or to communicate transmit the viewers. So to put it formally, we in data 100 call the specifically two goals for visualization. There's one goal that it will help you understand your data a little bit better. So this is going to be part of your exploratory data analysis. What you're basically doing here is that you try to make some visual of your data, some columns, to make sense of the data. And also, you're trying to make sure that the visuals that you're creating are very kind of flexible, lightweight, and then you can iteratively change it to different columns and quickly visualize the trends in your data. We don't want to spend too much time in here, but we also want to make sure that we have a visual sense of a summary of the data in one column or in a specific set of columns. And the goal to that we want to call out for is the communicating the results with the stakeholders. This is what we're going to spend a lot of time in the next lecture talking about how to effectively use visualization that are very descriptive. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat

leaf_chunk.audio: So the goal of general visualization, kind of going back to the data style cycle that I showed you is to kind of provide a high level description of the data or a high level visual of the data to ourselves or to communicate transmit the viewers. So to put it formally, we in data 100 call the specifically two goals for visualization. There's one goal that it will help you understand your data a little bit better. So this is going to be part of your exploratory data analysis. What you're basically doing here is that you try to make some visual of your data, some columns, to make sense of the data. And also, you're trying to make sure that the visuals that you're creating are very kind of flexible, lightweight, and then you can iteratively change it to different columns and quickly visualize the trends in your data. We don't want to spend too much time in here, but we also want to make sure that we have a visual sense of a summary of the data in one column or in a specific set of columns. And the goal to that we want to call out for is the communicating the results with the stakeholders. This is what we're going to spend a lot of time in the next lecture talking about how to effectively use visualization that are very descriptive.

children.title: Goals and Considerations in Data Visualization
children.summary: Data visualization serves two main goals: first, to aid personal understanding of data and results, which is crucial for exploratory data analysis, allowing for the summarization of trends visually before detailed analysis; second, to effectively communicate results and conclusions to others, requiring careful editorial choices to achieve specific communication goals. Key considerations include clarity, accessibility, and providing necessary context, emphasizing that visualizations should not merely be aesthetically pleasing but should thoughtfully convey ideas.

It has access labeled and whatnot. And what kind of visual should we use in order to effectively communicate the results of the model. So kind of thinking about these two goals, generally you can see that we're not going to be about making pretty plots, although we will make pretty plots. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Goals and Techniques in Data Visualization
children.summary: Effective data visualization requires understanding the audience and purpose, focusing on personal comprehension and clear communication of results. Visualizations should prioritize clarity, accessibility, and context over aesthetics. The first half of the course will cover selecting appropriate plot types for different variable types and generating these plots through coding. Key techniques include kernel density estimation for analyzing trends in distributions. The second half will focus on plot stylization, smoothing visual data, and enhancing context through labeling and color.

children.title: Goals and Considerations in Data Visualization
children.summary: Effective plotting requires pre-thinking about the audience and the purpose of the visualization, whether for personal understanding or stakeholder communication. The primary goals of data visualization include aiding personal comprehension of data and clearly communicating results to others. Visualizations should not just be aesthetically pleasing but should be carefully crafted to convey ideas effectively, considering clarity, accessibility, and context. The first half of the visualization topics will cover choosing the appropriate plot types for different variable types and generating these plots through coding.

children.title: Overview of Data Visualization Techniques
children.summary: The lecture covers the goals of data visualization, emphasizing that visualizations should effectively communicate ideas rather than just be aesthetically pleasing. Key topics include selecting the appropriate type of plot for different variable types, generating these plots through coding, and the introduction of a specific technique called kernel density estimation to analyze trends in distributions. The first half of the course will focus on choosing the right plots, while the second half will address stylizing plots, smoothing visual data, and providing context through labeling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in that sense, these two lectures are divided between the lecture one or today's lecture is just generally talking about different kinds of plots looking into different types of variables and then figuring out what is the appropriate type of plot that we can use for those visualizations. We will also try to learn a specific technique that you haven't seen before is called carnal density estimation in order to estimate trends in a distribution. And we're going to talk about what distribution is. But we're going to basically see how we can plot kind of a normalized way of histograms to make sense of the distribution of the trends in a data set. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Data Visualization Goals and Techniques Overview
children.summary: The upcoming lecture will focus on necessary transformations to prepare data for modeling, emphasizing that even clean data may not be in the desired format. The process involves iterative transformations and visualizations until the data meets the required specifications. Key goals of data visualization include making effective stylistic choices to communicate ideas rather than just creating visually appealing images. The first half of the visualization topics in the course covers selecting appropriate plots for different variable types, while the second half addresses plot stylization, context through labeling and color, and generating plots through coding.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In the second half, which is on Tuesday's lecture, we're going to come back and we're going to talk about different transformations that you need to do in order to make sure that your data is actually ready for the modeling. So today we're going to see that, okay, I'm going to do the visualization and although Although my data is clean, it is not in the format that necessarily I want. So that's the part that we have a loop in the Explorer 3D analysis phase that we go back to the transformation and then we plot again up until the point that it takes the form that we exactly want before we move on to the modeling phase. children.title: Understanding Data Visualization and Distributions
children.summary: The segment discusses the science behind visualization, specifically focusing on visualizing distributions within data sets. A distribution is defined as a summary of data, encompassing the set of values a variable can take and the frequency of each value's occurrence. The goals of data visualization are highlighted, emphasizing that visualizations should effectively communicate ideas rather than just being aesthetically pleasing. The first half of the visualization topics in the course will cover choosing the right plot and stylizing plots, while the second half will focus on smoothing and transforming visual data, providing context through labeling and color, and generating plots through code.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And we're going to talk about the science behind visualization and a little bit information channels how to utilize it effectively again in the next lecture. So to start by talking about the most frequent way or the kind of the task of visualization, which is visualizing distributions in your data set or in the data science life cycle. What is a distribution? To put it simply distribution is the summary of your data. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat

children.title: Understanding Distributions in Data Sets
children.summary: A distribution consists of two key components: the set of unique values a variable can take and the frequency of each value's occurrence. For example, when summarizing data about faculty at Cal, one must identify all unique departments represented in the dataset and count the number of faculty members in each department.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What kind of summary? This summary has two pieces. So before I define it as the summary has the set of unique values that a variable in your distribution can take. And then the frequency with which each one of them will come up. Right, so let me give you an example to make sense of what do I mean by that. Let's think about all faculty at Cal. Each faculty has a home department, right? So if you have a data set of faculty names and the department affiliations and whatnot, if you want to summarize this data set so that you make a distribution out of it, what you need to do is you need to make sure that you capture all the unique departments that exist in the data set, right? So that's where you are looking at the set of values in your distribution. And then what you're going to look at, additionally is that we need an additional piece of information which is what is the number of faculty that are basically housed within each one of those departments, right? children.title: Understanding Distributions and Proportions
children.summary: A distribution describes the set of values a variable can take and the frequency of each value's occurrence. In the context of faculty distribution across departments at Cal, the proportions of faculty affiliated with each department should sum to 100%, capturing the entire dataset. If using raw counts, these should equal the total number of data points. Examples will be provided to illustrate these concepts.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's the frequency with which each one of those departments have came up in your, have come up in your, um, in the entire distribution into the summarized entire data set as a distribution. What we will also make sure that we understand is that because we're looking at the distribution of all possible values in the data set, when we're looking at the proportion of faculty that are affiliated with each one of those departments, the summation of those proportions should add up to be 100 percent, because we're capturing everyone, right? children.title: Understanding Distributions in Data Sets
children.summary: A distribution describes the set of values a variable can take and the frequency of each value's occurrence. It is important for the data set to represent all departments, ensuring that the proportion of faculty in each department is zero or greater. The total counts or percentages of values must sum to the total size of the data set, reflecting accurate representation. An example discussed is the distribution of faculty across different departments at Cal, emphasizing the need for counts to match the total number of data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We also need to make sure that all departments are representative in our population, meaning that the probability or the proportion of faculty belonging to each individual department is gonna be. zero or greater than or equal to zero if you have a department that doesn't have a faculty for whatever reason that's going to be a missing value in your data set. The additional information is that we also need to make sure that if you're looking at all of the samples in our data set and we're looking at the allocation of each one of those samples into different different values in your set. you need to make sure that the sum of the total number of values that you have adds up to be equal to your entire data set size. children.title: Understanding Distributions and Bar Charts
children.summary: A distribution describes the set of values a variable can take and the frequency of each value. For example, the distribution of faculty across departments at Cal illustrates this concept. It is important to ensure that percentages sum to 100% when using proportions, or that counts equal the total number of data points when using raw counts. The segment emphasizes the importance of reading a plot's title and axis labels to determine if it effectively communicates the distribution.

The first way that you make sense of a plot is to read the title. And then you have to also take a look at the axis labels and make sure that you are understanding what's plotted on the x axis, what's plotted on the y axis. children.title: Teens' Social Media Trends and Data Visualization
children.summary: A survey reveals that about 49% of U.S. teens share accomplishments on social media, while 11% discuss religious beliefs and 9% political beliefs, with family (44%) and emotions/feelings (34%) being key topics. The discussion on distributions highlights that valid distributions require individuals to belong to one category, as shown in an income tier chart. In contrast, the social media habits chart is not a valid distribution due to multiple selections. The segment also explains variable types and visualizations, noting that bar plots are suitable for qualitative variables and can represent small counts of discrete quantitative variables, with bar lengths indicating proportions or percentages.

children.title: Teens' Social Media Posting Trends Overview
children.summary: A survey indicates that approximately 49% of U.S. teens share their accomplishments on social media, while only 11% discuss religious beliefs and 9% political beliefs. The survey permits multiple category selections, making it difficult to define a single distribution. Key topics shared by teens include family at 44% and emotions/feelings at 34%.

children.title: Teens' Social Media Posting Trends
children.summary: A survey reveals that about 49% of U.S. teens share their accomplishments on social media, with lower percentages discussing religious (11%) or political beliefs (9%). The survey allows multiple category selections, complicating the definition of a single distribution. Significant topics shared by teens include family (44%) and emotions/feelings (34%).

children.title: Teens' Social Media Posting Habits
children.summary: A survey indicates that approximately 49% of U.S. teens post their accomplishments on social media, while fewer discuss their religious (11%) or political beliefs (9%). The data suggests that respondents can select multiple categories for their posts, which complicates the notion of defining a single distribution. The findings highlight the diverse range of topics teens are willing to share online, with family (44%) and emotions/feelings (34%) also being significant categories.

children.title: Understanding Distributions and Variable Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses the characteristics of distributions in income and social media data, emphasizing that a valid distribution requires individuals to belong to one category, illustrated by an income tier chart where percentages sum to 100%. In contrast, a chart of teens' social media habits is not a distribution as respondents can select multiple options. It also covers types of variables and their visualizations, noting that quantitative variables differ from qualitative ones. Bar plots are identified as suitable for qualitative variables (both ordinal and nominal) and can represent small counts of discrete quantitative variables, with bar lengths indicating proportions or percentages.

children.title: Understanding Distributions and Variable Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses the characteristics of distributions in income and social media data, emphasizing that a valid distribution requires individuals to belong to one category, exemplified by an income tier chart where percentages sum to 100%. In contrast, a chart depicting teens' social media habits is not a distribution because respondents can select multiple options. Additionally, it covers the types of variables and their corresponding visualizations, noting that quantitative variables differ from qualitative ones. Bar plots are highlighted as suitable for qualitative variables (both ordinal and nominal) and can also represent small counts of discrete quantitative variables, with bar lengths indicating proportions or percentages.

children.title: Understanding Distributions in Income and Social Media Data
children.summary: The segment discusses whether certain charts represent distributions. It explains that a distribution requires individuals to belong to only one category, as seen in the income tier chart where percentages sum to 100%. In contrast, the chart on teens' social media habits shows percentages of individuals in multiple categories, indicating it is not a distribution since respondents can select multiple options.

children.title: Types of Variables and Bar Plots in Visualization
children.summary: Different types of variables require specific visualizations. Quantitative variables can be visualized differently than qualitative variables. Bar plots are primarily used for qualitative variables, both ordinal and nominal, and can also be applied to quantitative discrete variables when the number of instances is small. The length of the bars in a bar chart represents the proportion or percentage of individuals in each category, without utilizing the video information.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We can validate it and then every individual is captured in one of those income tiers, which is exactly what we want. So now going into a little bit deeper into actually making visualizations. So we know what is a distribution. We want to go ahead and plot distribution. But we can have different types of variables or different types of distributions that we can play with. In the last lecture, you'd seen that we have two general categories of variables. We have quantitative variables, and we need to have different visuals for different categories of different types of variables. And you're going to see why that's the case and why we think about the type of variable before making any of those visualizations. So we're going to start by bar plot. So bar plot is going to be mostly used when we're going to plot a qualitative variable, whether that be ordinal or nominal qualitative variable. And sometimes we might also use bar plot for quantitative discrete variables. And quantitative discrete because if the number of instances is as small, then we can technically think about those as small number of options of those discrete quantitative values to be also kind of categorized. And that's why we can also apply to that case. What you saw in the previous slide of call was a bar chart. So this was a bar chart, again, an pollinated variable. You can see that what we are encoding, thinking about information channels that you're receiving is that what we encoded was the lengths. So the lengths of each one of these bars is showing you the proportion of, or the percentage of individuals in each one of those categories. What you're also seeing is that we are not using the vids information in any way, right? Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: We can validate it and then every individual is captured in one of those income tiers, which is exactly what we want. So now going into a little bit deeper into actually making visualizations. So we know what is a distribution. We want to go ahead and plot distribution. But we can have different types of variables or different types of distributions that we can play with. In the last lecture, you'd seen that we have two general categories of variables. We have quantitative variables, and we need to have different visuals for different categories of different types of variables. And you're going to see why that's the case and why we think about the type of variable before making any of those visualizations. So we're going to start by bar plot. So bar plot is going to be mostly used when we're going to plot a qualitative variable, whether that be ordinal or nominal qualitative variable. And sometimes we might also use bar plot for quantitative discrete variables. And quantitative discrete because if the number of instances is as small, then we can technically think about those as small number of options of those discrete quantitative values to be also kind of categorized. And that's why we can also apply to that case. What you saw in the previous slide of call was a bar chart. So this was a bar chart, again, an pollinated variable. You can see that what we are encoding, thinking about information channels that you're receiving is that what we encoded was the lengths. So the lengths of each one of these bars is showing you the proportion of, or the percentage of individuals in each one of those categories. What you're also seeing is that we are not using the vids information in any way, right?

children.title: Bar Plots and Plotting Libraries Overview
children.summary: Bar plots effectively display distributions of qualitative variables and can incorporate quantitative discrete data, where lengths represent values and widths are non-informative. Colors can indicate sub-categories, such as income tiers among American adults. The World Bank dataset will be used to visualize trends, employing Matplotlib and Seaborn for plotting. A dataset of development statistics from 160 countries will be analyzed for continental trends, with both libraries sharing a similar plotting function template that requires importing the library and specifying sequences for the x-axis and y-axis items.

children.title: Bar Plots and Plotting Libraries Overview
children.summary: Bar plots are effective for displaying distributions of qualitative variables and can incorporate quantitative discrete data, where lengths represent values and widths are non-informative. Colors can indicate sub-categories, such as income tiers among American adults. The World Bank dataset will be utilized to visualize trends, employing Matplotlib and Seaborn for plotting. A dataset of development statistics from 160 countries will be analyzed for continental trends, with both libraries sharing a similar plotting function template, requiring the import of the library and sequences for the x-axis and y-axis items.

children.title: Bar Plots and Introduction to Plotting Libraries
children.summary: Bar plots are effective for displaying qualitative variable distributions and can include quantitative discrete data. In these plots, lengths represent values while widths are non-informative, and colors can denote sub-categories, exemplified by the income tiers of American adults. The World Bank dataset will be used for visualizing trends. Additionally, the lecture introduces Matplotlib and Seaborn for plotting, focusing on a dataset of development statistics from 160 countries to analyze continental trends. Both libraries follow a similar plotting function template, with Matplotlib requiring the import of the library and sequences for x-axis and y-axis items.

children.title: Bar Plots and World Bank Data Visualization
children.summary: Bar plots are commonly used to display the distribution of qualitative variables, sometimes including quantitative discrete data with few unique values. In these plots, lengths represent values while widths do not convey any information, and color can indicate sub-categories. An example discussed is the proportion of American adults across income tiers. The World Bank dataset will be utilized for visualizing various trends throughout the session.

children.title: Introduction to Plotting with Matplotlib and Seaborn
children.summary: A dataset containing development statistics for 160 countries will be used to analyze trends across continents. The lecture will focus on two main programming libraries for plotting: Matplotlib and Seaborn. Both libraries share a similar template for plotting functions, although the specific function names differ. The basic template for invoking a Matplotlib function involves importing the library and providing sequences for the x-axis and y-axis items to create various plots. The World Bank dataset will serve as the primary data source for the exercises.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is a data set that has 160 different countries and then it has different kinds of development statistics about different countries and it also has the content association of those countries. Why did I say content and association? Because think about the faculty association to each one of those departments that I talked about before. What I can do is I can plot the distribution of these countries in different continents, counting how many countries we have in each continent and then see the trends. So what we're going to see today and also on Tuesday is two main programming library that we'll be using for Plotting. You might have heard them, you might have seen us importing them in some of the notebooks. We're going to talk about MAT plotlib and seabourne. And the good thing about them is that each one of them follow a specific template for different plotting functions. It's just that the name of the function that plots different types of plots is going to be different, but the template for how we invoke them is going to be exactly the same, or mostly the same. The template for MAC plotlet is that it basically gets a sequence. What is a sequence? A sequence can be a list, it can be an array, it can be a series object, or what have you. And then it gets a sequence of items that needs to be plotted on the x-axis, it gets a sequence of items that needs to be plotted on the y-axis and it will go ahead them plot them and use the proper name of the plot that you want to plot. Pass the sequences, it will do its own magic. So the 10th foot for invoking any math plotlet function would be first to import math plotlet. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: So this is a data set that has 160 different countries and then it has different kinds of development statistics about different countries and it also has the content association of those countries. Why did I say content and association? Because think about the faculty association to each one of those departments that I talked about before. What I can do is I can plot the distribution of these countries in different continents, counting how many countries we have in each continent and then see the trends. So what we're going to see today and also on Tuesday is two main programming library that we'll be using for Plotting. You might have heard them, you might have seen us importing them in some of the notebooks. We're going to talk about MAT plotlib and seabourne. And the good thing about them is that each one of them follow a specific template for different plotting functions. It's just that the name of the function that plots different types of plots is going to be different, but the template for how we invoke them is going to be exactly the same, or mostly the same. The template for MAC plotlet is that it basically gets a sequence. What is a sequence? A sequence can be a list, it can be an array, it can be a series object, or what have you. And then it gets a sequence of items that needs to be plotted on the x-axis, it gets a sequence of items that needs to be plotted on the y-axis and it will go ahead them plot them and use the proper name of the plot that you want to plot. Pass the sequences, it will do its own magic. So the 10th foot for invoking any math plotlet function would be first to import math plotlet.

children.title: Introduction to Matplotlib and Seaborn Visualization Techniques
children.summary: This segment introduces Matplotlib and Seaborn for data visualization in Data 100, explaining the structure of plotting functions and the standard import statement. It demonstrates accessing and displaying data from the World Bank dataset using pandas, focusing on bar plots to visualize the distribution of the 'Continent' column through the .value_counts() function. The transition from the datascience library in Data 8 to Matplotlib and Seaborn in Data 100 is noted, along with a code example for creating a horizontal bar plot of continent distribution. The importance of labeling axes and titles is emphasized, including a correction of a typo in the x-axis label function. It highlights that bar plots are suitable for qualitative variables, while Seaborn's countplot may result in excessive bars for quantitative data. Additionally, the functionality of the PLT library for data visualization, including value order and naming arguments, is discussed.

children.title: Introduction to Matplotlib and Seaborn Visualization Techniques
children.summary: This segment introduces Matplotlib and Seaborn for data visualization in Data 100, explaining the structure of plotting functions and the standard import statement. It demonstrates how to access and display data from the World Bank dataset using pandas, focusing on bar plots to visualize the distribution of categorical variables, specifically the 'Continent' column, through the .value_counts() function. The transition from the datascience library in Data 8 to Matplotlib and Seaborn in Data 100 is noted, with a code example for creating a horizontal bar plot of continent distribution. The segment also covers the creation of bar plots, emphasizing the importance of labeling axes and titles, correcting a typo in the x-axis label function, and using Pandas for value counts. It highlights that bar plots are suitable for qualitative variables, while Seaborn's countplot may lead to excessive bars for quantitative data. Finally, it discusses the functionality of the PLT library for data visualization, including value order and naming arguments.

children.title: Introduction to Matplotlib and Visualization Techniques
children.summary: The segment introduces Matplotlib and Seaborn for plotting in Data 100, explaining the structure of plotting functions and the standard import statement. It demonstrates visualization techniques using the World Bank dataset, detailing how to access data with pandas and display the data frame's head. Bar plots are highlighted for visualizing the distribution of categorical variables, specifically the 'Continent' column, using the .value_counts() function to analyze country distribution. The transition from the datascience library in Data 8 to Matplotlib and Seaborn in Data 100 is noted, with a code example for generating a horizontal bar plot of continent distribution.

children.title: Introduction to Matplotlib for Plotting
children.summary: The lecture discusses the use of Matplotlib and Seaborn for generating plots in Data 100. It emphasizes the structure of Matplotlib plotting functions, where a sequence of values is passed for the x-axis and another for the y-axis. The standard import statement for Matplotlib is shown as 'import matplotlib.pyplot as plt', and the format for calling plotting functions is outlined as 'plt.plotting_function(x_values, y_values)'. An example dataset is introduced for practical application.

children.title: Visualization Techniques with World Bank Dataset
children.summary: The lecture demonstrates visualization techniques using the World Bank dataset, which contains development statistics for 166 countries across 47 columns. The dataset is accessed using pandas, and the head of the data frame is displayed. Bar plots are introduced as a method to visualize the distribution of categorical variables, specifically focusing on the 'Continent' column, with the use of the .value_counts() function to analyze the number of countries per continent.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'm loading the word bank dataset. It's also uploaded on your student repo. So if we take a look at the head of this Data set, what you're seeing is that, okay, all of those, the development statistics that we saw, we have 47 different columns, and then I'm showing you the head of this distribution, or this data frame. What you're seeing is that I have 166 different countries, and again, for all of those 47 columns that we have. So what I want to do next is I want to go ahead and actually. from datascience import Table

t. barh("index", "Continent")

from datascience import Table

children.title: Calculating and Visualizing Country Distribution by Continent
children.summary: The segment discusses how to calculate the distribution of countries across different continents using a data frame. It highlights the use of the 'value_counts' method to analyze the 'Continent' column and visualize the results with bar plots. The transition from using the datascience library in Data 8 to employing Matplotlib and Seaborn for visualizations in Data 100 is noted, with a code example provided for generating a horizontal bar plot of the continent distribution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Calculate the distribution of countries that I have in different continents. How do I calculate the distribution? You remember that we had value accounts, right? So what I can say is I can say that I have a board bank data frame. I'm going to go ahead and grab the continent. um the continent column and i would say value counts and we will be able to see the trend. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Bar Plots for Categorical Data Distribution
children.summary: Bar plots are utilized to display distributions of categorical variables, specifically for the 'Continent' column in the dataset. The process involves using the .value_counts() method to determine the number of countries per continent. In Data 100, visualizations will be created using Matplotlib and Seaborn libraries. An example code snippet demonstrates how to generate a horizontal bar plot for the 'Continent' data using the datascience library, transitioning to Matplotlib for future visualizations.

children.title: Creating Bar Plots with Matplotlib
children.summary: A series object is created to store data from the 'Continent' column, which will be used to generate a bar plot. The syntax for using Matplotlib's plotting function is discussed, specifically the 'plt.bar()' function. The x-axis will represent different continents, using the index of the series object, while the y-axis will display the corresponding values. The necessary sequences for both axes are identified as 'continents.index' for the x-axis and 'continents.values' for the y-axis.

children.title: Suppressing Output in Plotting Functions
children.summary: When plotting in Python, omitting a semicolon at the end of the plotting function can result in additional output being displayed, such as a description of the plot object created. To avoid this, it's recommended to include a semicolon, which suppresses unnecessary text and displays only the plot. Additionally, it's important to set axis labels and a title for clarity.

This is because if you don't put a semicolon at the end of the last line of your plotting function, it will basically try to plot or print out whatever the last element that you plotted was. So here it's telling me that it created a bar, continue, continue object and just talks about something about that specific object that we don't necessarily want. children.title: Creating Bar Plots with Matplotlib
children.summary: In Data 100, the lecture focuses on using Matplotlib and Seaborn libraries for data visualization. A bar plot is generated using the Matplotlib function plt.bar, which displays the count of different countries categorized by continents. It's emphasized that when creating plots, it's essential to communicate all relevant information clearly, including axis labels and a title to ensure the viewer understands the data presented.

children.title: Setting Up Bar Plot with Labels
children.summary: The segment discusses how to create a bar plot using matplotlib in Python, specifically focusing on setting the x-axis label to 'Continents' and the y-axis label to indicate a count of countries. It also covers adding a title to the plot, which describes the distribution of countries. Additionally, it highlights the importance of concluding the plotting function with a semicolon to suppress unnecessary output, explaining that omitting it would result in extraneous text being displayed alongside the plot.

children.title: Creating Bar Graphs with Matplotlib, Pandas, and Seaborn
children.summary: This segment covers how to create bar plots using Matplotlib, Pandas, and Seaborn. It begins with plotting the distribution of countries across continents using Matplotlib, emphasizing the need for proper labeling of axes and titles, and correcting a typo in the function for setting the x-axis label. The use of Pandas for creating bar plots through value counts from a 'continent' column is demonstrated, along with the importance of including a semicolon in the plt.bar call to suppress unnecessary output. The segment highlights that bar plots are suitable for qualitative variables, while using Seaborn's countplot for quantitative variables can lead to an excessive number of bars due to the continuous nature of the data. Finally, it discusses the PLT library's functionality for data visualization, including the correct order of values and naming arguments for clarity.

children.title: Plotting Bar Graphs with Matplotlib
children.summary: The segment discusses how to create a bar plot using Matplotlib, specifically plotting the distribution of countries across different continents. It emphasizes the importance of labeling the x-axis and y-axis, as well as adding a title to the plot. An error is noted due to a typo in the function name for setting the x-axis label, which is corrected to 'plt.xlabel' from 'plt.xabel'.

Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what

children.title: Creating Bar Plots with Pandas and Seaborn
children.summary: Demonstrated how to use native pandas plotting to create a bar plot by calculating value counts from a 'continent' column. Explained the importance of including a semicolon in the plt.bar call to suppress unnecessary output, and emphasized setting axis labels and titles for clarity. Discussed the appropriate use of bar plots for qualitative variables and illustrated the limitations of using sns.countplot for quantitative variables, highlighting that continuous variables can result in an excessive number of bars.

Note that we concluded our call to plt. bar with a semicolon ( ; ). This suppresses any unnecessary output other than the plot. If we do not include

pandas native plotting:
[ ]: # pandas native plotting
Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]:
wb ['Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' ]
[ ]:
sns. countplot (data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016')
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.

children.title: Pandas and Seaborn Bar Plotting Techniques
children.summary: Pandas native plotting can be utilized to create bar plots using the value_counts method, with plt.title used to set the plot title, such as 'Distribution of Countries'. Including a semicolon at the end of the plt.bar command suppresses extraneous output. It's emphasized that bar plots are suitable for visualizing qualitative variables. Using Seaborn's countplot for a quantitative variable, like gross national income per capita, leads to many bars for each unique value, which is problematic as continuous variables do not have a finite number of values.

Note that we concluded our call to plt. bar with a semicolon ( ; ). This suppresses any unnecessary output other than the plot. If we do not include
a semicolon, the plot will still generate, however, we will see extraneous text as well:
We also remember to set the axis labels and the title for the plot.
pandas native plotting:
[ ] :
# pandas native plotting
wb["Continent"] . value_counts() . plot(kind = "bar")
Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]: wb[ 'Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016']
[ ]:
sns . countplot (data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016')
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.

Note that we concluded our call to plt. bar with a semicolon ( ; ). This suppresses any unnecessary output other than the plot. If we do not include
a semicolon, the plot will still generate, however, we will see extraneous text as well:
We also remember to set the axis labels and the title for the plot.
pandas native plotting:

Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]: wb[ 'Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016']
[ ]:
sns . countplot (data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016')
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.

children.title: Using PLT for Data Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of the PLT library for data visualization, specifically how to invoke plotting functions, pass sequences of values, and label axes and titles. It emphasizes the importance of maintaining the correct order of values when plotting, with x-values first and y-values second. Additionally, it mentions the option to name arguments to avoid confusion in case of forgetting the order.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Under the hood map plot lib is invoked. So that's why I always have access to PLT in order to control the access labels and put the title and whatnot. So going back to Verriver, so I'm going to restart this and kind of going over exactly the same template that we saw. We imported, we call the plotting function, we pass the sequences of values, we can label them, we extend that, we can call x label, y label, title, set up, send all of those values to the plotting. We can exactly what you saw was directly called PLT dot bar, plot the stuff that we want, And we want to make sure that the order of the values that we're passing is exactly x first and then y second or alternatively you could say that what if I forget the order of passing the values in those cases you could make sure that you're naming your argument. children.title: Generating Bar Plots with Matplotlib and Pandas
children.summary: The segment discusses how to create visualizations using Matplotlib and Seaborn in Data 100. It explains the process of generating a bar plot with the Matplotlib function plt.bar by first creating a Series object for continents using value_counts. The syntax for plotting includes specifying the x and y values, setting axis labels, and adding a title to the plot, which illustrates the distribution of countries across different continents.

children.title: Introduction to Seaborn and Count Plots
children.summary: Seaborn is a high-level plotting interface built on Matplotlib that simplifies the plotting process by allowing users to pass entire DataFrames and specify column labels for axes. To use Seaborn, import it with 'import seaborn as sns'. Bar plots can be generated using Seaborn's count plot function, which automatically handles data counting, coloring, and axis labeling. Users need to specify the dataset and the x-axis variable, such as 'continent', and can add titles using PLT functions. This showcases Seaborn's ease of use for creating visually appealing plots.

children.title: Introduction to Seaborn for Plotting
children.summary: Seaborn is a high-level interface built on top of Matplotlib that simplifies the plotting process by inferring styles and functionalities by default. It allows users to pass an entire DataFrame and specify the column labels for the x-axis and y-axis, making it easier to interpret plots without relying on integer positions.

leaf_chunk.audio: What was the framework exactly called whatever function and then pass the sequence of values and then we'll be plotting that. So everything is going to make sense and fall into place. I'm now moving on to the C board. I want to tell you that C-born is just the wrapper on top of matplotlet. But it's just an easier framework and a higher level language to work with. It does a lot of the functionalities by default for us, so we don't actually need to do a lot of stylization that we want to do or do. Doing the value, you can somewhat not in order to calculate the counts of different, different items. items. So it infers a lot of the style designs from the function that we're invoking. But under the hood, it's actually a matte, lovely library that's plotting stuff. So think about just your learning one language, but you're learning to work with one framework with two different interfaces. With the C-born, the way that we're invoking it is slightly different. The template for us this time with C-born is that we pass a data frame and then we pass the label for the columns that we want to plot on the x-axis and on the y-axis. So we work with labels. You remember that I was telling you that we will work a lot with the labels and not necessarily with integer positions. This is one of those reasons that it's easier to interpret what we're plotting using this plot function.

children.title: Generating Bar Plots with Seaborn
children.summary: To import the Seaborn library, use the alias 'sns' with the command 'import seaborn as sns'. Seaborn functions require passing an entire DataFrame and specifying the columns to plot. For labeling axes and adding titles, utilize PLT.xlabel, PLT.ylabel, and PLT.title. A practical example involves creating a count plot using Seaborn, demonstrating the structure of the plotting function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: How do we import C-Born? We basically say import C-Born as SNS and that's the usual or usual alias that we use for C-Born. So the SNS stop-plotting function, passing the entire data frame, passing the columns that you want to plot, and that's exactly what we'll be doing. How do we deal with labeling axes and then put a title? We know that it's basically under the hood and Macbublib plot that It is being drawn technically, so what we can do is we can use the same control. Use PLT.x label, PLT.y label, PLT.tidal in order to put labels and put title and move on. Okay. So let's take a look at what it looks like for us to do exactly the same bar plot kind of plotting that we were doing, but this time doing it with Seaborn. So what I'll do is I'll say that SNS, I already imported it at the top, I would say go ahead and plot a count plot. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]: wb[' Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' ]
[ ] :
sns. countplot(data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' )
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:
.
Histogram
Box plot
· Violin plot
Box Plots and Violin Plots

Seaborn is typically given the alias sns
import seaborn as sns
sns.plotting_function(data=df, x="x_col", y="y_col")
To add labels and a title, use the same syntax as before:
plt.xlabel("x axis label")
plt.ylabel ("y axis label")
plt. title("Title of the plot");

Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]: wb[' Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' ]
[ ] :
sns. countplot(data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' )
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:
.
Histogram
Box plot
· Violin plot
Box Plots and Violin Plots

children.title: Using Seaborn's Count Plot for Visualization
children.summary: The count plot function in Seaborn simplifies the creation of bar plots by automatically handling data counting, coloring, and axis labeling. Users can pass a dataset, such as WB, and specify the x-axis variable, like 'continent'. Seaborn generates a visually appealing plot with minimal input, requiring only a title to be added manually. This functionality highlights Seaborn's user-friendly features for data visualization.

Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]:
wb [ 'Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016 ']
[ ]:
sns. countplot(data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' )
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:
Histogram
Box plot
· Violin plot
Box Plots and Violin Plots

# Seaborn countplot

Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]:
wb [ 'Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016 ']
[ ]:
sns. countplot(data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' )
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:
Histogram
Box plot
· Violin plot
Box Plots and Violin Plots

children.title: Visualizing Quantitative Variables with Plots
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of count plots for visualizing quantitative variables, specifically gross national income (GNI), and highlights the limitations of bar plots for quantitative data due to issues like overplotting. It emphasizes that count plots can yield misleading results for quantitative distributions. Alternative visualization methods such as box plots and violin plots are introduced, with box plots showing the middle 50% of data and quartiles, while violin plots provide additional density information. Key elements of box plots are explained, including the concept of quartiles, where Q1 represents the 25th percentile and Q2 the median at the 50th percentile.

children.title: Using Count Plots for Quantitative Variables
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the application of count plots to visualize quantitative variables, specifically gross national income (GNI). It emphasizes that bar plots are traditionally used for qualitative (categorical) variables and raises the question of what occurs when attempting to use a count plot for a quantitative variable like GNI per capita.

unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:

children.title: Limitations of Bar Plots for Quantitative Data
children.summary: Bar plots are suitable for visualizing qualitative variable distributions but not effective for quantitative values due to issues like overplotting, which makes it hard to read individual data points. When using count plots for quantitative data, such as gross national income per capita, the results can be misleading, as most bars may represent values that appear only once. This highlights the need for alternative visualization methods for quantitative distributions.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If I go ahead and just do the count plot on it, you saw that it took a long time for it to generate it, because under the hood it's doing all of those value count calculation itself, and then it created this count plot for me, which really doesn't make sense. I can make sense of what data is being presented here. But if you take a look for all of those instances that are really overplotted on the x-axis, that's an example that we're calling it overplotting, because I can't really read each one of those positions for each one of those bars. And then the height of the bars, you're seeing that for most cases, it's equal to one, right? which means that that was a value that only appeared once. So that's not the right way of visualizing quantitative values. So this is one of the reasons that we say bar plot is really good for qualitative, qualitative variable distribution, but not necessarily for quantitative ones. But if I had just only a few different quantitative values that I could actually have nice bars, then I could probably use that. So that's kind of the caveat here that we will try to be a verus. So we saw this camp plot. It was nice. We saw that for a quantitative distribution. It might not be helpful. And now we want to see what is the helpful way to visualize the distribution of a quantitative variable. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat >

leaf_chunk.audio: If I go ahead and just do the count plot on it, you saw that it took a long time for it to generate it, because under the hood it's doing all of those value count calculation itself, and then it created this count plot for me, which really doesn't make sense. I can make sense of what data is being presented here. But if you take a look for all of those instances that are really overplotted on the x-axis, that's an example that we're calling it overplotting, because I can't really read each one of those positions for each one of those bars. And then the height of the bars, you're seeing that for most cases, it's equal to one, right? which means that that was a value that only appeared once. So that's not the right way of visualizing quantitative values. So this is one of the reasons that we say bar plot is really good for qualitative, qualitative variable distribution, but not necessarily for quantitative ones. But if I had just only a few different quantitative values that I could actually have nice bars, then I could probably use that. So that's kind of the caveat here that we will try to be a verus. So we saw this camp plot. It was nice. We saw that for a quantitative distribution. It might not be helpful. And now we want to see what is the helpful way to visualize the distribution of a quantitative variable.

Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.

What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:

children.title: Box Plots and Violin Plots Overview
children.summary: The discussion focuses on box plots and violin plots as methods for visualizing data distributions. Box plots display the trend of data distribution, indicating that the middle 50% of data lies within the box. Key elements of box plots will be explored. Violin plots, which include the density function, provide additional information by showing the proportion of data points at specific values on the Y-axis, contrasting with box plots that only indicate aggregate data without detailing point counts.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we're going to talk about in the next part. Box plot, violin plot, and histograms. You'd seen histograms a lot, but here we're going to basically see what information you can gather from histograms and how can we summarize information in histograms in today's lecture. So let's start with boxblood and violent plot. Simple syntax. You call sns.boxblood or you call sns.violent plot and you will get the boxblood and violent plot. What you're seeing is that generally they communicate the same set of information. What we're seeing is that the boxblood is generally showing you the trend of the distribution of your data. It shows me that the middle 50% of our data is within that box. And then we're gonna talk about different elements that are visualized in the box plot and kind of make sense of what is visualized there. Violin plot is very similar, but it also has the additional channel that this widths of this violin part is also encoding some information. It is a density function on top of a box plot, so we will tell you that what is the probability or what's the proportion of the data points that are exactly at any given point on that Y axis that you're going up, right? Whereas the box plot basically tells you that There are some points here, but I'm not going to tell you how many of each do you have, right? But I'm just going to tell you that on aggregate, 50% of your data is within the box. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat

leaf_chunk.audio: So we're going to talk about in the next part. Box plot, violin plot, and histograms. You'd seen histograms a lot, but here we're going to basically see what information you can gather from histograms and how can we summarize information in histograms in today's lecture. So let's start with boxblood and violent plot. Simple syntax. You call sns.boxblood or you call sns.violent plot and you will get the boxblood and violent plot. What you're seeing is that generally they communicate the same set of information. What we're seeing is that the boxblood is generally showing you the trend of the distribution of your data. It shows me that the middle 50% of our data is within that box. And then we're gonna talk about different elements that are visualized in the box plot and kind of make sense of what is visualized there. Violin plot is very similar, but it also has the additional channel that this widths of this violin part is also encoding some information. It is a density function on top of a box plot, so we will tell you that what is the probability or what's the proportion of the data points that are exactly at any given point on that Y axis that you're going up, right? Whereas the box plot basically tells you that There are some points here, but I'm not going to tell you how many of each do you have, right? But I'm just going to tell you that on aggregate, 50% of your data is within the box.

children.title: Understanding Box Plots and Quartiles
children.summary: Box plots and violin plots are used to display data distributions, focusing on quartiles. A quartile divides a sorted dataset into four equal parts, with Q1 representing the 25th percentile, indicating that 25% of the data falls below this point. The median, or Q2, marks the 50th percentile, where half of the data is below this value. The distance between Q1 and Q2 represents a quarter of the dataset. In box plots, the width of the box does not convey any information, while in violin plots, the width indicates the density of data points at each value.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's move on and talk a little bit about What is encoded in box, blood and violent blood? So essentially box, blood is plotting, quartiles. What is a quartile? Core distance for four. So quartile means that we have a data set. We're going to sort it and we're going to chunk it into quarters. Right? So when we chunk it into quarters, then what we have is that I'm looking at the distribution. And I'm going to say that if I look at the lower 25% of my data, or what we call 25th percentile, what is the point or what is the quartile that we're going to call a Q1 that encodes that value? That Q1 is the point that 25 percent or first quartile is the point that 25 percent of the data is on it or below it. median. So basically this here and this point is our Q2 or median, which basically says that I have 50% of the data on or below this exact point. So the distance between Q1 and Q2 is obviously a quarter, right? And the distance from all the weight from the minimum up until Q1 is 25% right or a quarter of your data set. leaf_chunk.audio: So let's move on and talk a little bit about What is encoded in box, blood and violent blood? So essentially box, blood is plotting, quartiles. What is a quartile? Core distance for four. So quartile means that we have a data set. We're going to sort it and we're going to chunk it into quarters. Right? So when we chunk it into quarters, then what we have is that I'm looking at the distribution. And I'm going to say that if I look at the lower 25% of my data, or what we call 25th percentile, what is the point or what is the quartile that we're going to call a Q1 that encodes that value? That Q1 is the point that 25 percent or first quartile is the point that 25 percent of the data is on it or below it. median. So basically this here and this point is our Q2 or median, which basically says that I have 50% of the data on or below this exact point. So the distance between Q1 and Q2 is obviously a quarter, right? And the distance from all the weight from the minimum up until Q1 is 25% right or a quarter of your data set.

children.title: Understanding Quartiles and Interquartile Range
children.summary: The upper quartile (Q3) represents the 75th percentile, indicating that 75% of the data falls below this point. This concept is essential for analyzing data distribution by dividing it into four segments, allowing for an understanding of the data's spread. The focus is on the interval containing the middle 50% of the data, defined by the first quartile (Q1) and third quartile (Q3). The interquartile range (IQR), calculated as Q3 minus Q1, measures this spread, highlighting the central tendency of the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then similarly we also have upper profile or Q3 which is also 75% which basically says that 75% of the data is on or below this exact point. Which means that 25% is above it. Right? That's how you can think about chunking your data set. When you sort it, you chunk it into four different pieces and you're basically looking at the trend in your data. What's important when we are understanding these partials is that we want to figure out what is the spread of our data. What we're interested in other words is that we want to see what is the interval that carries the middle 50% of our data. We want to make sure that our data is centralized as much as possible so that the majority of our data or the 50% of the middle 50% of our data is very centered. leaf_chunk.audio: And then similarly we also have upper profile or Q3 which is also 75% which basically says that 75% of the data is on or below this exact point. Which means that 25% is above it. Right? That's how you can think about chunking your data set. When you sort it, you chunk it into four different pieces and you're basically looking at the trend in your data. What's important when we are understanding these partials is that we want to figure out what is the spread of our data. What we're interested in other words is that we want to see what is the interval that carries the middle 50% of our data. We want to make sure that our data is centralized as much as possible so that the majority of our data or the 50% of the middle 50% of our data is very centered.

children.title: Understanding Interquartile Range and Box Plots
children.summary: The interquartile range (IQR) is defined as the difference between the third quartile (Q3) and the first quartile (Q1), indicating the spread of the middle 50% of data. A smaller IQR suggests that data points are more centered around the median (Q2), while a larger IQR indicates a wider distribution. Box plots visually represent this information, showing Q1, Q2, and Q3. A smaller box in a box plot indicates a more concentrated data set, whereas a larger box reflects a more dispersed set of values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: meaning that the range between Q1 and Q3 is as small as possible. What do we call this rent? We call this rent interquartile rent, which is effectively Q3 minus Q1. So this interquartel range is showing you the spread of the data. It basically tells you that, okay, if interquartel range is 10 units, it will tell you that 50% of your data is within 10 units. So you will basically have some kind of a spike up distribution in that ten units of the values in your distribution. If your inter quartet range is equal to five. In order to fit 50% of the data into that range, you need to have a sharp risk bike. So that's basically showing you how centered your data is going to be. The larger this intercarter range is going to be, it's basically going to show you that your data is more spreaded out. The smaller this value is, it will show you that your data is more centered around, or between around I would say Q2 or median and so on and so forth. So what is included in the box plot is exactly that information. The box shows you Q1, it will show you Q2 or median and it will show you Q3, right? So that's where the box is. So that basically means that if you have a smaller box, your data is more centered. If you have a larger or wider box, that means that your data has a wider distribution, so your data points are more spreaded out in the sense. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X
