leaf_chunk.audio: meaning that the range between Q1 and Q3 is as small as possible. What do we call this rent? We call this rent interquartile rent, which is effectively Q3 minus Q1. So this interquartel range is showing you the spread of the data. It basically tells you that, okay, if interquartel range is 10 units, it will tell you that 50% of your data is within 10 units. So you will basically have some kind of a spike up distribution in that ten units of the values in your distribution. If your inter quartet range is equal to five. In order to fit 50% of the data into that range, you need to have a sharp risk bike. So that's basically showing you how centered your data is going to be. The larger this intercarter range is going to be, it's basically going to show you that your data is more spreaded out. The smaller this value is, it will show you that your data is more centered around, or between around I would say Q2 or median and so on and so forth. So what is included in the box plot is exactly that information. The box shows you Q1, it will show you Q2 or median and it will show you Q3, right? So that's where the box is. So that basically means that if you have a smaller box, your data is more centered. If you have a larger or wider box, that means that your data has a wider distribution, so your data points are more spreaded out in the sense.

children.title: Interquartile Range, Outliers, and Data Visualization
children.summary: The segment covers the interquartile range (IQR) in relation to box plots, detailing how to determine reasonable limits for data by defining the center between the first quartile (Q1) and the third quartile (Q3). It explains that outliers are data points outside the range defined by 1.5 times the IQR above Q3 and below Q1, indicating they likely do not belong to the same distribution. Key components of box plots, including whiskers and quartiles (Q1, Q2, Q3), are discussed, with an example visualizing GDP growth for 2016. The importance of visualizing data with box plots and violin plots is highlighted, noting that box plots summarize distributions based on Gaussian distribution, while violin plots show density functions alongside quartiles. The syntax for creating these plots in Python using Seaborn is also provided, along with specifications for datasets and axes.

children.title: Interquartile Range and Box Plots
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of interquartile range (IQR) in relation to box plots, explaining how to determine reasonable limits for data. It highlights that the center of the data can be defined between the first quartile (Q1) and the third quartile (Q3), with a typical range extending 1.5 times the IQR below Q1 and above Q3 to identify outliers. The speaker emphasizes the importance of this range for ensuring data is from the same distribution without including outliers, referencing empirical calculations and Gaussian distribution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what are those kind of viscures that are at the top or at the bottom? What we would say is that empirically, we calculated and we said that a reasonable range of the data that has the center between or center 50% between Q1 and Q3 can be spreaded out. 1.5 times inter-clot, 1.5 times inter-clot, RTL range below Q1 and 1.5 times interquart TL range above Q3. So let's take a look one more time. We would say that if this is my center of the data, this is very 50% of the data is. empirically using some kind of a notion of Gaussian distribution, you can basically say that if I want to be sure that my data is from the same distribution and I'm not including all the outliers, I can extend these legs and I can say that I can go for the duration of 1.5 times interquarty range. below the q1 and 1.5 times inter quartile range above q3 and that's a reasonable range. children.title: Understanding Outliers and Box Plots
children.summary: Data points that fall outside the reasonable range, defined by upper and lower thresholds, are considered outliers and unlikely to belong to the same distribution. The segment also discusses box plots, highlighting key components such as the whiskers, which represent the upper quartile plus 1.5 times the interquartile range (IQR) and the lower quartile minus 1.5 times the IQR. The quartiles are defined as Q1 (25th percentile), Q2 (median), and Q3 (75th percentile). An example is provided using a box plot to visualize gross domestic product growth for 2016.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That reasonable range means that I'm sure with 99.97% that my data should be within this especially grunge between the upper viscayr and the lower viscayr. Anything below it and anything below the lower risk or anything above that per risk is going to be an outlier. That means that it's very unlikely that those data sets or data points are from the same distribution or the same trend that we'd seen. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Box Plots and Violin Plots Overview
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of visualizing quantitative data using box plots and violin plots. Box plots summarize the distribution of data based on the Gaussian distribution, ensuring that data points are within the lower and upper whiskers with 99.7% probability. Violin plots enhance this by displaying the density function along with quartiles (Q1, Q2, Q3), providing insights into the probability distribution of values between quartiles. The syntax for creating these plots in Python is outlined, using 'sns.boxplot' or 'sns.violinplot' from the Seaborn library, along with specifications for the dataset and axes.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'm not going to go over why 1.5 times interquartar range, but I want if you're interested, go and take a look at this link. It's basically an empirical way of saying that how can we make sure that we are summarizing the distribution based on the assumption that the distribution is a Gaussian distribution and make sure that any data point that we see any data point that we sample from this distribution is going to be within the lower viscure and upper viscure with 99.7. percent probability. So that's a summary of the box plot. So that's why we need to visualize our quantitative values using a box plot because this basically summarizes the entire trend. I don't need to know individual values, but I just need to know the distribution of it. a violin plot. plots, box plot, and on top of that, it shows you the density function on the curves. So if you look closely, what you're seeing here is that I have Q2 sitting here in the middle, I have Q3, and then I also have Q1, and I have upper viscuers and also lower viscuers here. But what you're seeing is that it will also show you that, okay, not only that you have Q1 and Q2 at those places, but let me tell you what's the probability distribution of the values between Q1 and Q2 so that you know how it is spreaded out in between. So it's kind of a nice way of getting more information. Again, the syntax of how to plot violin plot or box plot is to just simply use S and S dot box plot or S and S dot violin plot. You pass the dataset or the data frame and then you specify which axis you want to plot or which column you want to plot and which axis you want to plot it on. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: So I'm not going to go over why 1.5 times interquartar range, but I want if you're interested, go and take a look at this link. It's basically an empirical way of saying that how can we make sure that we are summarizing the distribution based on the assumption that the distribution is a Gaussian distribution and make sure that any data point that we see any data point that we sample from this distribution is going to be within the lower viscure and upper viscure with 99.7. percent probability. So that's a summary of the box plot. So that's why we need to visualize our quantitative values using a box plot because this basically summarizes the entire trend. I don't need to know individual values, but I just need to know the distribution of it. a violin plot. plots, box plot, and on top of that, it shows you the density function on the curves. So if you look closely, what you're seeing here is that I have Q2 sitting here in the middle, I have Q3, and then I also have Q1, and I have upper viscuers and also lower viscuers here. But what you're seeing is that it will also show you that, okay, not only that you have Q1 and Q2 at those places, but let me tell you what's the probability distribution of the values between Q1 and Q2 so that you know how it is spreaded out in between. So it's kind of a nice way of getting more information. Again, the syntax of how to plot violin plot or box plot is to just simply use S and S dot box plot or S and S dot violin plot. You pass the dataset or the data frame and then you specify which axis you want to plot or which column you want to plot and which axis you want to plot it on.

children.title: Plotting GDP and Violin Plots Overview
children.summary: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is plotted along the y-axis, emphasizing the control over the orientation of the plot. The discussion introduces violin plots, which are similar to box plots but also incorporate smoothed density curves. The significance of the width of the box in violin plots is highlighted, along with the presence of three quartiles and whiskers.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So effectively here I'm saying that I want to plot GDP, right? So this is gross domestic product and I'm saying that I want to plot that along the y-axis because I am only using one dimension effectively. If I would say x equal to GDP, it would give me a violent plot that was horizontal. So that's kind of how you can control whether you want to do a box let horizontally over vertically. children.title: Violin Plots vs Box Plots in Data Visualization
children.summary: Violin plots serve as an alternative to box plots by displaying not only the quartiles but also smoothed density curves of the data distribution. This allows for a better understanding of the distribution's shape and the density of data points between quartiles, enhancing insight into the data's characteristics, such as peaks and spread. The width of the violin plot indicates the density of data points, providing more information than a traditional box plot.

children.title: Understanding Box and Violin Plots
children.summary: Box plots summarize data distributions, while violin plots provide additional smoothed density curves, enhancing the interpretation of data. The width of the violin plot indicates density, and both plots retain the quartiles and whiskers. The discussion also includes how to incorporate qualitative variables to compare distributions of quantitative variables across different categories.

children.title: Creating Side-by-Side Box and Violin Plots
children.summary: To visualize GDP across different continents, side-by-side box plots or violin plots can be used. By specifying the continents on the x-axis and the GDP values on the y-axis, tools like Seaborn will automatically group the data and calculate necessary values, resulting in a colorful plot that displays multiple distributions simultaneously. Additionally, qualitative variables can be incorporated to compare the distribution of a quantitative continuous variable across different qualitative categories, enhancing the analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Say, for example, I want to show the distribution of GDP for different continents. So for each individual continent, I want to have a box plot that that box plot shows GDP for data-specific continent. So I can use side by side, side by side box blood or side by side violent blood. You don't need to do any control over it. You just need to specify that, OK, on the x-axis, I want different continents. It will go ahead and just do group by ag and whatnot. And it will calculate all of those values. That's the beauty of C-born. And then on the y-axis, you're saying that, oh, you want the box bloods actually, or the box blood distribution values. And that's why you're calling the box blood. under Y axis I have all of those GDP values plotted and it will basically give you this colorful plot. multiple values can be presented and plotted at the same time. children.title: Understanding Histograms and Density Calculation
children.summary: Histograms summarize quantitative data by grouping values into 'bins,' where the height of each bin indicates the percentage of data points it contains. The area of a bin corresponds to this percentage, with an example showing a bin width of $16,410 and height of 4.77 x 10^-5 representing approximately 78.3% of total data points. The concept of density is introduced, which approximates the probability distribution for value ranges. Different plotting libraries like Matplotlib and Seaborn can be utilized to create histograms, each with unique syntax.

children.title: Understanding Histograms and Data Representation
children.summary: Histograms are used to summarize quantitative data by grouping similar values into shared 'bins.' The height of each bin represents the percentage of data points it contains, with the area of the bin corresponding to this percentage. For example, a bin with a width of $16,410 and a height of 4.77 x 10^-5 indicates it contains approximately 78.3% of the total data points in the dataset.

children.title: Understanding Histograms and Density Calculation
children.summary: A histogram organizes quantitative data into bins, allowing for visualization of the distribution of values. Each bin represents a range of values, and the area of each bin corresponds to the percentage of data points it contains. For instance, the first bin's area can represent 78% of the total data points, calculated by multiplying the bin's width by its height. This concept is referred to as density, which approximates the probability distribution for specific value ranges. Different plotting libraries like Matplotlib and Seaborn can be used to create histograms, each with slightly different syntax.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So think about histogram as a way of us sorting the entire quantity, the list of quantitative values that we have, bin them, right? So you can basically see that there is a bin for anything between 0 and for example, 17,000 and so on and so forth. And total, we have five different bins. And then we are calculating or we're plotting these bars in a way that the area actually encodes the percentage. So for example, in this case, what we're seeing is that for this very first bin, the percentage of data points that we have in the first bin is equal to the area of that bin. So we're calculating which times height and this is going to give us 78%. So 78% of the data in the first bin and so on and so forth. The way that we encode the height is such that We arrive at the area being 78 percent. That's what we call density. Right? It's not exactly the probability, but it's a value that when you calculate the area of that bin, you get at the probability distribution for a single value or for a single range of values. There are different ways that we can plot histograms using map bloodlub using seaborne. It's slightly different syntax, mostly kind of map bloodlub use his function. leaf_chunk.audio: So think about histogram as a way of us sorting the entire quantity, the list of quantitative values that we have, bin them, right? So you can basically see that there is a bin for anything between 0 and for example, 17,000 and so on and so forth. And total, we have five different bins. And then we are calculating or we're plotting these bars in a way that the area actually encodes the percentage. So for example, in this case, what we're seeing is that for this very first bin, the percentage of data points that we have in the first bin is equal to the area of that bin. So we're calculating which times height and this is going to give us 78%. So 78% of the data in the first bin and so on and so forth. The way that we encode the height is such that We arrive at the area being 78 percent. That's what we call density. Right? It's not exactly the probability, but it's a value that when you calculate the area of that bin, you get at the probability distribution for a single value or for a single range of values. There are different ways that we can plot histograms using map bloodlub using seaborne. It's slightly different syntax, mostly kind of map bloodlub use his function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Seaborne uses his plot function. and um What we have again at the normal kind of setup for calling a map level function pass x values and basically say that okay now go ahead and encode all of these x values and undivided access we say that we want the density to be plotted as opposed to the count because if you don't provide it it will basically give you that okay you want it to have x values I'm gonna I'll just give you the count. But here you're saying that I want density so that there is scale in a way that the area is proportional to the probability of the data point. For the hist plot, we are using exactly the same kind of trend passing the data frame passing the column that we want to actually plot. plot, and then we are also saying that the statistic that we want to plot is going to be is density instead of count. So that's kind of the control that you have. I'll leave it to you to go ahead and take a look at the documentation because there are different controls that you have about what you want to plot on the Vi-Axis and so on and so forth. You can also count... Create, define the number of bins that you want to have in each one of the categories. Instead of the function itself deciding how many bins you want to have. You could directly say I want five bins, I want ten bins and so on and so forth. So we could also have overlayed histograms and the same way that we had side by side box plots in order to encode two variables. If we have two variables that we want to encode, we could actually use the color or the hue information in order to do that. So what I plotted here is that I calculated whether a country is in the north-sand hemisphere or southern hemisphere and I basically said that okay I want to look at the GNI or the north-sand hemisphere countries versus the southern hemisphere countries and I want to look at the distribution. leaf_chunk.audio: Seaborne uses his plot function. and um What we have again at the normal kind of setup for calling a map level function pass x values and basically say that okay now go ahead and encode all of these x values and undivided access we say that we want the density to be plotted as opposed to the count because if you don't provide it it will basically give you that okay you want it to have x values I'm gonna I'll just give you the count. But here you're saying that I want density so that there is scale in a way that the area is proportional to the probability of the data point. For the hist plot, we are using exactly the same kind of trend passing the data frame passing the column that we want to actually plot. plot, and then we are also saying that the statistic that we want to plot is going to be is density instead of count. So that's kind of the control that you have. I'll leave it to you to go ahead and take a look at the documentation because there are different controls that you have about what you want to plot on the Vi-Axis and so on and so forth. You can also count... Create, define the number of bins that you want to have in each one of the categories. Instead of the function itself deciding how many bins you want to have. You could directly say I want five bins, I want ten bins and so on and so forth. So we could also have overlayed histograms and the same way that we had side by side box plots in order to encode two variables. If we have two variables that we want to encode, we could actually use the color or the hue information in order to do that. So what I plotted here is that I calculated whether a country is in the north-sand hemisphere or southern hemisphere and I basically said that okay I want to look at the GNI or the north-sand hemisphere countries versus the southern hemisphere countries and I want to look at the distribution.

children.title: Overlaid Histograms and Distribution Analysis
children.summary: Overlaid histograms facilitate the comparison of a quantitative variable's distribution across qualitative categories, using color coding to represent different groups, such as hemispheres. Understanding right and left tail distributions is crucial, with right tail distributions having longer tails to the right, indicating less representation of larger values, and left tail distributions having longer tails to the left. The skewness of a histogram reveals its tail direction, with right skew indicating a long right tail and left skew indicating a long left tail. Additionally, analyzing histogram trends is essential for ensuring normal distribution and avoiding skewness, while the concept of mode is introduced to describe the number of peaks in a distribution, emphasizing its significance for future modeling discussions.

children.title: Overlaid Histograms for Distribution Comparison
children.summary: Overlaid histograms allow for the comparison of a quantitative variable's distribution across qualitative categories by plotting two histograms on top of each other, each represented by different colors. In this example, the color attribute is derived from the hemisphere column, with orange representing the Northern Hemisphere and blue for the Southern Hemisphere. A legend is created to provide contextual information about the color coding. The purpose of plotting histograms is to analyze the distribution of the quantitative variable, which has not been covered in previous courses.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It plots one histogram with one color, it plots another histogram on top of that that's why we call it overlayed histogram with two different colors. You You can see that the color attribute is going to come from the hemisphere column that I created. So it basically says that, okay, I'm going to segment them based on hemisphere. I'm going to basically have the orange one for Northern part and then the blue one for the southern part. And additionally, what they will do is it will create this legend for us. Most of the time, if color is involved in any way, we might want to add legend so that be at that additional contact, contextual information about what's flooded in orange and what's flooded in blue. And that's called a legend. So, some more kind of information that you can gather from histograms. These are the things that you've not seen in data 8. Looking at the histogram and the reason that we plot histogram is to figure out that the kind of the distribution of the quantitative variable that we have. leaf_chunk.audio: It plots one histogram with one color, it plots another histogram on top of that that's why we call it overlayed histogram with two different colors. You You can see that the color attribute is going to come from the hemisphere column that I created. So it basically says that, okay, I'm going to segment them based on hemisphere. I'm going to basically have the orange one for Northern part and then the blue one for the southern part. And additionally, what they will do is it will create this legend for us. Most of the time, if color is involved in any way, we might want to add legend so that be at that additional contact, contextual information about what's flooded in orange and what's flooded in blue. And that's called a legend. So, some more kind of information that you can gather from histograms. These are the things that you've not seen in data 8. Looking at the histogram and the reason that we plot histogram is to figure out that the kind of the distribution of the quantitative variable that we have.

children.title: Understanding Right and Left Tail Distributions
children.summary: A right tail distribution has a long tail extending to the right, indicating that larger values are less represented in the data but can still influence modeling. Conversely, a left tail distribution has a long tail extending to the left. The skew of a histogram indicates the direction of its tail: a long right tail signifies right skew, a long left tail signifies left skew, and a symmetric histogram shows no clear skew.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, here on the left, you're seeing that there is a long tail in the right side of the distribution. We call it a right tail distribution. So a right tail distribution is going to be probabil, probabilismatically because when we are going to do the modeling, they're going to be large values that are less represented in the data, but they still exist and they might bias your model in some way. So we need to fix all of these issues before getting into the modeling. In the other part, you see a long left tail, we call it a left-discute distribution. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Understanding Histogram Trends and Mode
children.summary: The focus is on analyzing histogram trends to ensure data is normally distributed, avoiding left or right skewness. The concept of mode is introduced, explaining how it indicates peaks in a distribution: unimodal for one peak, bimodal for two, and multi-modal for more than two peaks. The importance of summarizing mode information is emphasized for future lectures and modeling discussions, highlighting the need to understand the distribution's characteristics.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we are really interested in is look at the trend, look at the histogram and make sure that our data is not left-discute or right-discute in a sense, and our data is kind of normally distributed so that we can work with it is slightly better. We're gonna go into the details of what kind of issues that they can create and how to fix these issues in the next lecture and also in the modeling lectures to come. The additional information that we can gather is mode. Mode is basically showing you whether the peaks in your distribution or whether the peaks in your histogram. We can have one peak, we call it unimodal, we can have two peaks, we call it bi-modal, we can have more than one peak, but we don't go and say try-modal and whatnot, we would just call it multi-modal data sets. So this kind of mode information is going to be exactly what we might want to gather and we might want to summarize in our data. But we're going to see why that's important and why we need to summarize it a little bit more to make sure that we understand the mode of our distribution understanding whether it's unimodal or bymodal in future lectures and also in modeling lectures, particularly. leaf_chunk.audio: What we are really interested in is look at the trend, look at the histogram and make sure that our data is not left-discute or right-discute in a sense, and our data is kind of normally distributed so that we can work with it is slightly better. We're gonna go into the details of what kind of issues that they can create and how to fix these issues in the next lecture and also in the modeling lectures to come. The additional information that we can gather is mode. Mode is basically showing you whether the peaks in your distribution or whether the peaks in your histogram. We can have one peak, we call it unimodal, we can have two peaks, we call it bi-modal, we can have more than one peak, but we don't go and say try-modal and whatnot, we would just call it multi-modal data sets. So this kind of mode information is going to be exactly what we might want to gather and we might want to summarize in our data. But we're going to see why that's important and why we need to summarize it a little bit more to make sure that we understand the mode of our distribution understanding whether it's unimodal or bymodal in future lectures and also in modeling lectures, particularly.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Histogram Binning Techniques
children.summary: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) is introduced as a method for visualizing data distributions by creating a smooth representation that summarizes data trends without displaying the entire dataset. The concept is compared to placing fabric over data points, where the fabric's characteristics influence visibility of data peaks. The significance of histogram binning is highlighted, showing how varying the number of bins can change interpretations of data distributions, such as unimodal, bimodal, or multimodal representations. KDE involves summing normalized kernels to approximate a probability distribution, with the choice of bandwidth affecting the visual output; a bandwidth of 0.65 is noted for its aesthetic appeal. Techniques for layering KDE plots with histograms are discussed, including the use of the 'displot' function from the seaborn library to visualize distributions effectively, exemplified by plotting gross national income per capita.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Histogram Binning
children.summary: Kernel density estimation (KDE) is introduced as a technique for summarizing data trends by visualizing the overall shape and distribution without displaying the entire dataset. This method is likened to placing a fabric over data points, where the characteristics of the fabric, such as thickness and texture, affect the visibility of peaks in the data. The importance of histogram binning is discussed, highlighting how the number of bins can alter the interpretation of data distributions: five bins may indicate unimodality, ten bins suggest bimodality, and twenty bins could point to multimodality. KDE is explained as a way to create a smooth representation of distributions by assigning a probability function to each data point through a kernel, which helps in visualizing trends. The process of constructing a probabilistic model is also covered, emphasizing the aggregation of observations and the need for each kernel function to ensure positivity and normalization for a valid probability distribution.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation Overview
children.summary: Kernel density estimation is introduced as a technique for summarizing data trends without showing the entire dataset. It is compared to placing a fabric over data points to visualize the overall shape and trends, facilitating an understanding of the distribution while minimizing detail. The characteristics of the fabric, such as thickness and texture, influence the visibility of various peaks in the data, enhancing the representation of the dataset's trends.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation Concept Overview
children.summary: Kernel density estimation is introduced as a method for summarizing trends in data without displaying the entire dataset. It is likened to placing a fabric over data points to visualize the overall shape and trends, allowing users to understand the distribution without overwhelming detail. The thickness and texture of the fabric can affect the visibility of different peaks in the data, providing a clearer representation of the dataset's trends.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's get back. 5 4 3 2 And one, so, kernel-lensity estimation. It's a very cool topic. I want you to think about kernel-lensity estimate and why we need to do that. As, like, for example, I have two things on a table. I have two data points, right? And I want to summarize the trends in my data point, or I have like multiple different bars. And I want to summarize it, but I don't want to show the entire data set or data points that I have to the user so that they make sense of what's happening. What I can do is I can put a fabric on top of all of these data points and then show them the trend or the shape of that fabric. So depending on the thickness of that fabric, they might even only see the tallest one or they might see if the fabric is a little bit lighter in texture. They might also to see some of the details of the other peaks or other data points that I have in my trend. So I want you to think about kernel density estimation as a way that we're trying to... Put kind of a fabric on top of our bars in a histogram and make sense of how can we normalize or how can we give a general visual about what's happening in the trend of our dataset. leaf_chunk.audio: So let's get back. 5 4 3 2 And one, so, kernel-lensity estimation. It's a very cool topic. I want you to think about kernel-lensity estimate and why we need to do that. As, like, for example, I have two things on a table. I have two data points, right? And I want to summarize the trends in my data point, or I have like multiple different bars. And I want to summarize it, but I don't want to show the entire data set or data points that I have to the user so that they make sense of what's happening. What I can do is I can put a fabric on top of all of these data points and then show them the trend or the shape of that fabric. So depending on the thickness of that fabric, they might even only see the tallest one or they might see if the fabric is a little bit lighter in texture. They might also to see some of the details of the other peaks or other data points that I have in my trend. So I want you to think about kernel density estimation as a way that we're trying to... Put kind of a fabric on top of our bars in a histogram and make sense of how can we normalize or how can we give a general visual about what's happening in the trend of our dataset.

children.title: Histogram Binning and Kernel Density Estimation
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of binning in histograms, noting how the number of bins influences data distribution interpretation; five bins reveal a unimodal distribution, ten bins may suggest bimodality, and twenty bins could indicate multimodality. It also covers kernel density estimation (KDE) as a method for creating a smooth representation of distributions, where each data point is assigned a probability function through a kernel, enhancing visualization of data trends. Additionally, it explains constructing a probabilistic model by aggregating observations and modeling each data point with a kernel function, ensuring positivity and normalization to achieve a proper probability distribution.

children.title: Histogram Binning and Distribution Analysis
children.summary: The importance of binning in histograms is discussed, highlighting how the number of bins can affect the interpretation of data distributions. With five bins, a unimodal distribution is observed, while increasing the bins to ten may suggest a bimodal distribution. Further increasing the bins to twenty could indicate a multi-modal distribution. The segment emphasizes the need for careful consideration of bin numbers when analyzing data distributions.

Seaborn allows us to easily create side-by-side plots by specify both an x and y column.
[ ]:
sns. boxplot(data=wb, x="Continent", y='Gross domestic product: % growth : 2016' )
plt. title("The distribution of gross domestic product for different continents");
Histograms
You are likely familiar with histograms from Data 8. A histogram collects continuous data into bins, then plots this binned data. Each bin reflects the
density of datapoints with values that lie between the left and right ends of the bin.
[ ]:
# The edgecolor' argument controls the color of the bin edges
gni = wb["Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016"]
plt.hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white")
# Add Labels
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
[ ] :
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016", stat="density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
We can overlay histograms (or density curves) to compare distributions across qualitative categories.
The hue parameter of sns. histplot specifies the column that should be used to determine the color of each category. hue can be used in many
Seaborn plotting functions.
Notice that the resulting plot includes a legend describing which color corresponds to each hemisphere - a legend should always be included if color
is used to encode information in a visualization!

Each bin of a histogram is scaled such that its area is equal to the percentage of all datapoints that it contains.
[ ]:
densities, bins, _ = plt. hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white", bins=5)
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt. title("A histogram of the distribution of GNI per capita");
print(f"First bin has width {bins [1]-bins[0]} and height {densities[0]}")
print (f"This corresponds to {bins [1]-bins [0]} * {densities [0]} = {(bins[1]-bins[0]) *densities[0]*100}% of the data")
In Data 100, we describe a "mode" of a histogram as a peak in the distribution. Often, however, it is difficult to determine what counts as its own
"peak." For example, the number of peaks in the distribution of HIV rates across different countries varies depending on the number of histogram bins
we plot.
[ ]:
# Rename the very Long column name for convenience
wb = wb. rename(columns={'Antiretroviral therapy coverage: % of people living with HIV: 2015' : "HIV rate"})
# With 5 bins, it seems that there is only one peak
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=5)
plt.title("5 histogram bins");
[ ]:
# With 10 bins, there seem to be two peaks
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=10)
plt.title("10 histogram bins");
[ ]:
# And with 20 bins, it becomes hard to say what counts as a "peak"!
sns.histplot(data=wb, x ="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=20)
plt.title("20 histogram bins");

As this example illustrates, it is sometimes more useful to understand the general structure of our data, rather than focus on individual observations.
Kernel density estimation helps with this goal.
Kernel Density Estimation
Kernel density estimation (KDE) allows us to "smooth" a distribution to display general trends and eliminate noisy, distracting detail.
[19]:
# The smooth curve overlaid on the histogram is a KDE
sns. displot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", kde=True, stat="density")
plt.title("Histogram and overlaid KDE on HIV rate distribution");

Seaborn allows us to easily create side-by-side plots by specify both an x and y column.
[ ]:
sns. boxplot(data=wb, x="Continent", y='Gross domestic product: % growth : 2016' )
plt. title("The distribution of gross domestic product for different continents");
Histograms
You are likely familiar with histograms from Data 8. A histogram collects continuous data into bins, then plots this binned data. Each bin reflects the
density of datapoints with values that lie between the left and right ends of the bin.
[ ]:
# The edgecolor' argument controls the color of the bin edges
gni = wb["Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016"]
plt.hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white")
# Add Labels
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
[ ] :
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016", stat="density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
We can overlay histograms (or density curves) to compare distributions across qualitative categories.
The hue parameter of sns. histplot specifies the column that should be used to determine the color of each category. hue can be used in many
Seaborn plotting functions.
Notice that the resulting plot includes a legend describing which color corresponds to each hemisphere - a legend should always be included if color
is used to encode information in a visualization!

As this example illustrates, it is sometimes more useful to understand the general structure of our data, rather than focus on individual observations.
Kernel density estimation helps with this goal.
Kernel Density Estimation

As this example illustrates, it is sometimes more useful to understand the general structure of our data, rather than focus on individual observations.
Kernel density estimation helps with this goal.
Kernel Density Estimation

Seaborn allows us to easily create side-by-side plots by specify both an x and y column.
[ ]:
sns. boxplot(data=wb, x="Continent", y='Gross domestic product: % growth : 2016' )
plt. title("The distribution of gross domestic product for different continents");
Histograms
You are likely familiar with histograms from Data 8. A histogram collects continuous data into bins, then plots this binned data. Each bin reflects the
density of datapoints with values that lie between the left and right ends of the bin.
[ ]:
# The edgecolor' argument controls the color of the bin edges
gni = wb["Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016"]
plt.hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white")
# Add Labels
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
[ ] :
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016", stat="density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
We can overlay histograms (or density curves) to compare distributions across qualitative categories.
The hue parameter of sns. histplot specifies the column that should be used to determine the color of each category. hue can be used in many
Seaborn plotting functions.
Notice that the resulting plot includes a legend describing which color corresponds to each hemisphere - a legend should always be included if color
is used to encode information in a visualization!

Each bin of a histogram is scaled such that its area is equal to the percentage of all datapoints that it contains.
[ ]:
densities, bins, _ = plt. hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white", bins=5)
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt. title("A histogram of the distribution of GNI per capita");
print(f"First bin has width {bins [1]-bins[0]} and height {densities[0]}")
print (f"This corresponds to {bins [1]-bins [0]} * {densities [0]} = {(bins[1]-bins[0]) *densities[0]*100}% of the data")
In Data 100, we describe a "mode" of a histogram as a peak in the distribution. Often, however, it is difficult to determine what counts as its own
"peak." For example, the number of peaks in the distribution of HIV rates across different countries varies depending on the number of histogram bins
we plot.
[ ]:
# Rename the very Long column name for convenience
wb = wb. rename(columns={'Antiretroviral therapy coverage: % of people living with HIV: 2015' : "HIV rate"})
# With 5 bins, it seems that there is only one peak
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=5)
plt.title("5 histogram bins");
[ ]:
# With 10 bins, there seem to be two peaks
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=10)
plt.title("10 histogram bins");
[ ]:
# And with 20 bins, it becomes hard to say what counts as a "peak"!
sns.histplot(data=wb, x ="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=20)
plt.title("20 histogram bins");

As this example illustrates, it is sometimes more useful to understand the general structure of our data, rather than focus on individual observations.
Kernel density estimation helps with this goal.
Kernel Density Estimation
Kernel density estimation (KDE) allows us to "smooth" a distribution to display general trends and eliminate noisy, distracting detail.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Probability Functions
children.summary: Kernel density estimation is a method used to create a smooth representation of a distribution, similar to a histogram but with enhanced smoothness. This involves assigning a probability function to each data point, which reflects the likelihood of sampling from areas of the distribution that are more densely populated. The kernel function serves as the probability function, indicating the chances of sampling the same data point or points slightly higher or lower in value. This approach helps visualize the general trend of the data distribution.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So because the decision that we're making about the mode of the distribution and sometimes also about the skewness of the distribution is going to be controlled by this kind of arbitrary variable, we might want to go ahead and say that I'm going to smooth this entire distribution and I'm going to give you a kernel density estimation. And a kernel density estimation is again, It tends to defunction that is kind of similar to the histogram, but it's more smooth. And how do we do this smoothness or making our plots or our curve more smooth? We will basically use the idea that I'm going to assume that every time that you want to sample a sample data point from your entire distribution and you go out in the valve, it's more likely that you will sample from the bars that are more represented in your distribution and you're going to sample less from the bins that are not represented that much. So I'm going to basically put a probability. this probability function on top of each one of the data points and then I'll sum up all of those probability functions and scale them in order to make sense of what's the general trend what's the probability that my data point is going to be sampled if I go at a sample again I'm going to sample a data point from the exact same distribution. So that was a love of complicated, but let me tell you. How we build a probabilistic notion into visualizing a distribution. So the core part of the kernel density estimation or how we're going to make this estimation is based on a kernel function. What is a kernel function? The kernel function says that I'm the probability function. And this probability function represents that what is the likelihood that every time that you sample a data point you will basically exactly get the same data point or you will get the data point that is slightly higher in value or is slightly lower in value. Say for example, I have the hiv rate sample one data point with the value equal to 20. In order for me to model the probability or the likelihood for me to be able to sample this data point again, I would say that there is a probabilistic word that is it is more likely for me to sample exactly the same data point. So there is a high probability here or a high density here when I'm going to basically say what's the probability that I'm going to get the same data point and as you increase the value or as you decrease the value. It is less likely less likely or less probable for you to sample those data points. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat >

leaf_chunk.audio: So because the decision that we're making about the mode of the distribution and sometimes also about the skewness of the distribution is going to be controlled by this kind of arbitrary variable, we might want to go ahead and say that I'm going to smooth this entire distribution and I'm going to give you a kernel density estimation. And a kernel density estimation is again, It tends to defunction that is kind of similar to the histogram, but it's more smooth. And how do we do this smoothness or making our plots or our curve more smooth? We will basically use the idea that I'm going to assume that every time that you want to sample a sample data point from your entire distribution and you go out in the valve, it's more likely that you will sample from the bars that are more represented in your distribution and you're going to sample less from the bins that are not represented that much. So I'm going to basically put a probability. this probability function on top of each one of the data points and then I'll sum up all of those probability functions and scale them in order to make sense of what's the general trend what's the probability that my data point is going to be sampled if I go at a sample again I'm going to sample a data point from the exact same distribution. So that was a love of complicated, but let me tell you. How we build a probabilistic notion into visualizing a distribution. So the core part of the kernel density estimation or how we're going to make this estimation is based on a kernel function. What is a kernel function? The kernel function says that I'm the probability function. And this probability function represents that what is the likelihood that every time that you sample a data point you will basically exactly get the same data point or you will get the data point that is slightly higher in value or is slightly lower in value. Say for example, I have the hiv rate sample one data point with the value equal to 20. In order for me to model the probability or the likelihood for me to be able to sample this data point again, I would say that there is a probabilistic word that is it is more likely for me to sample exactly the same data point. So there is a high probability here or a high density here when I'm going to basically say what's the probability that I'm going to get the same data point and as you increase the value or as you decrease the value. It is less likely less likely or less probable for you to sample those data points.

children.title: Building a Probabilistic Model with Kernels
children.summary: A probabilistic model can be constructed by aggregating observations from a dataset, treating them as ground truths. Each data point is modeled with a probability function, represented by a kernel, which indicates the likelihood of observing that sample. The kernel function ensures all values are positive and the area under the curve equals 1, reflecting a proper probability distribution. The goal is to apply this kernel to all data points in the dataset, summing the individual probability functions to create a comprehensive probability distribution for the entire dataset.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's how I'm going to build a probabilistic model. Right. So this basically says that based on all of your observations that you have, if you're assuming that your observations and your data set are your ground truths, and you're building a probability function on top of each individual data point that you have, you sum up or aggregate all of this information together, then you have a probability function that models the entire distribution. What you see in the kernel is how we can model the the probability or the likelihood of observing one sample or one row in your data frame or in your data set. But what we basically want to do is do exactly the same thing for all of the data points that we have in our data frame. So what you're seeing in this kernel is that this kernel is a probability function. All of the values starting from zero all the way to that 4D that I show they're all positive value. Right? So that's what we want from a probability. You can go ahead and calculate that the area under the care is equal to 1. Right? And it makes sense kind of for us to model this exact data point with HIV rate equal to 20 using this bill care function because it's more likely for you to get something that you think Think is your ground trust as you went out in the, in the world and you collected the data. And as you go to 21 to 22 or 30, then the probability of you observing those samples is going to drop, right? So that's basically what we're basically modeling with this kernel function. But exactly what we want is to model the entire data set, not only a single data point. So what we'll do is we'll basically say that let me put the kernel on top of all of the individual data points that I have, right? Because each one of them should be modeled with a probability function. And let me sum up all of those probability functions in order to build a probability distribution for the entire data set that I have. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: That's how I'm going to build a probabilistic model. Right. So this basically says that based on all of your observations that you have, if you're assuming that your observations and your data set are your ground truths, and you're building a probability function on top of each individual data point that you have, you sum up or aggregate all of this information together, then you have a probability function that models the entire distribution. What you see in the kernel is how we can model the the probability or the likelihood of observing one sample or one row in your data frame or in your data set. But what we basically want to do is do exactly the same thing for all of the data points that we have in our data frame. So what you're seeing in this kernel is that this kernel is a probability function. All of the values starting from zero all the way to that 4D that I show they're all positive value. Right? So that's what we want from a probability. You can go ahead and calculate that the area under the care is equal to 1. Right? And it makes sense kind of for us to model this exact data point with HIV rate equal to 20 using this bill care function because it's more likely for you to get something that you think Think is your ground trust as you went out in the, in the world and you collected the data. And as you go to 21 to 22 or 30, then the probability of you observing those samples is going to drop, right? So that's basically what we're basically modeling with this kernel function. But exactly what we want is to model the entire data set, not only a single data point. So what we'll do is we'll basically say that let me put the kernel on top of all of the individual data points that I have, right? Because each one of them should be modeled with a probability function. And let me sum up all of those probability functions in order to build a probability distribution for the entire data set that I have.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation Process Overview
children.summary: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) aims to approximate the probability distribution that generated a dataset. The process involves placing a kernel at each data point and normalizing these kernels so that the total area equals one. Each kernel is a function designed to capture the randomness of the sampled data. An example is provided using a fake dataset with five data points, where a Gaussian kernel with a specified bandwidth is placed at each point. Further definitions of the Gaussian kernel and bandwidth will be provided later.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But the caveat that when you're looking at the entire probability distribution of your data set, you need to make sure that the area under the care over the entire distribution should be equal to one. As opposed to here, the probability the area under the care for this care and all for a single data point is equal to one. So That's kind of only the middle part that we'll try to find a solution for. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Modeling Data Points with Gaussian Kernels
children.summary: A Gaussian kernel is placed at each individual data point in a dataset to model the data. An example with five data points is presented, illustrating how to visualize these points using a Gaussian kernel with a bandwidth of alpha = 1. The precise definitions of the Gaussian kernel and bandwidth will be provided in upcoming slides.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So again, because I want to model each individual data point, but I can do is I can go ahead and say that that nice bell-carved shape, which is the Gaussian kernel, I'm going to put it on each individual data point that I have. So here is kind of a random toy example with five different data points that I created. We can plot something like this, which has indicators for different data points using something that's called rock blood. So we're going to see rock flood also more in the next lecture. children.title: Modeling Data with Gaussian Kernels
children.summary: The discussion focuses on transforming a dataset into a probabilistic view by plotting its probability distribution. It introduces the concept of placing a Gaussian kernel at each data point, using a fake dataset with five collected datapoints as an example. The bandwidth of the Gaussian kernel is set to alpha = 1, and it is noted that the definitions of the Gaussian kernel and bandwidth will be provided in upcoming slides. Each line in the visual representation corresponds to a datapoint, illustrating how this modeling technique can be applied.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But I have indicators for each one of those data points, 2.1, and so on and so forth. I want to turn this data set into something that I can understand from a probabilistic view. And I want to plot the probability distribution over this entire distribution that I have. So modeling each individual, one of them using a kernel is going to basically give me all of these five gousines that I have. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Modeling Data Points with Gaussian Kernels
children.summary: To model individual data points, Gaussian kernels are placed at each point in a dataset. Summing these kernels helps to understand the overall probability distribution, especially in regions where data points are close together. This aggregation allows for a clearer interpretation of the data's behavior, as it indicates the likelihood of obtaining values between the individual points. A specific example involves a dataset with five data points and the application of a Gaussian kernel with a bandwidth of alpha = 1.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So five gousines, if I take a look at each individual, one of these gousines, then that's basically how we model each individual data point. But in order for me to model the entire thing, I want to summarize or sum up all of these galsunes. Why do we sum up all of these galsunes? Because, for example, in this specific area, that I have two points that are very close to each other, or in this specific area that I have two data points that are close to each other. I want to say that, okay, I have one sample here, I have one sample here, this is the probability distribution for this one, this is for our distribution for this either one. Looking at this region, it's very likely that you will get one of these two values or a value between these two values That's why I need to sum up all of those kernels in order to just basically make sense of what's happening in aggregate The only caveat is that I have five data points, right? Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: So five gousines, if I take a look at each individual, one of these gousines, then that's basically how we model each individual data point. But in order for me to model the entire thing, I want to summarize or sum up all of these galsunes. Why do we sum up all of these galsunes? Because, for example, in this specific area, that I have two points that are very close to each other, or in this specific area that I have two data points that are close to each other. I want to say that, okay, I have one sample here, I have one sample here, this is the probability distribution for this one, this is for our distribution for this either one. Looking at this region, it's very likely that you will get one of these two values or a value between these two values That's why I need to sum up all of those kernels in order to just basically make sense of what's happening in aggregate The only caveat is that I have five data points, right?

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation Basics
children.summary: The area under each kernel curve is equal to one, and when summed, five kernels yield a total area of five. To create a valid probability distribution, the height of each kernel must be adjusted to one fifth of its original height, resulting in each kernel having an area of one when combined. The discussion includes placing a Gaussian kernel with a bandwidth of alpha = 1 over a fake dataset consisting of five data points, with the Gaussian kernel and bandwidth to be defined in upcoming slides.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then each one of these kernels, the area under these curves is equal to one. If I sum up all of these areas, it will sum up to be equal to five. And five is not going to give me a probability distribution. of these kernel functions so that the heights are one fifths of their original height, so that the area under the curve of each individual one of them is one fifths, which then if I combine five of them together it will give me area under the curve of equal to one. children.title: Kernel Density Estimation Process and Visualization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on kernel density estimation (KDE) using a dataset of five data points. It explains that the probability of selecting a sample from a data frame of size N is represented as one over N, which reflects the maximum probability of obtaining a sample. The area under the curve for each data point is scaled to one over N, leading to the construction of individual kernels. The bandwidth, set to one, determines the shape of the Gaussian kernel, affecting its width and height. The resulting KDE reveals the distribution of data points, highlighting two peaks that indicate clusters of data, with contributions from the data points influencing the overall density estimate.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: dividing it by N? It's because you're saying that okay if I go in the Vile and Sample. It is one over I'm getting one over and probability that I'm going to get this sample in a data frame of n samples. So that's basically what you're doing right and then one over n is the maximum probability that you'll get. So that's why the area not that maximum probability or the share of a given data point that you have. So that's why it makes sense for the area under the cares for each individual point point to be one over n. So I'm going to scale it. A scaling down here is going to give me all of these kernels, which then I can go ahead and sum them up together and then get this kernel density estimate. So that's the entire process. So without looking at the data, I give you five data points. If you plot kernel density estimation and you plot it with the assumption that your bandwidth is equal to one, which is going to be the shape of that bell curve, which is kind of controlling how narrow or how wide that bell curve is going to be. This is going to be the trend that you're getting, which kind of makes sense. What you're expecting here is that you have two peaks here and then this data point contributes more to these first two samples and these two samples are kind of more cluster together. So that's where one mode comes here and another higher mode comes before it because it gets the contribution from the third data point in the middle. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Visualization Techniques
children.summary: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) is introduced as a method for visualizing data distributions by summing normalized kernels to create a smooth curve that approximates a probability distribution. The KDE curve is plotted using 'sns.kdeplot(points, bw_method=0.65)', alongside a histogram for density representation. The choice of bandwidth significantly affects the visualization, with a bandwidth of 0.65 chosen for its visual appeal, resulting in a bell curve shape. The segment also covers layering KDE plots with histograms, detailing how to plot both simultaneously and customize histogram parameters for density representation. Additionally, the 'displot' function from the seaborn library is discussed as a versatile tool for visualizing distributions, with an example demonstrating its application in plotting the distribution of gross national income per capita.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) Overview and Plotting
children.summary: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) is introduced as a method to visualize data distributions by summing normalized kernels to create a smooth curve approximating a probability distribution. The KDE curve is plotted using 'sns.kdeplot(points, bw_method=0.65)', alongside a histogram for density representation. The segment explains how the choice of bandwidth affects the visualization, with a bandwidth of 0.65 chosen for visual appeal, resulting in a bell curve shape. It also discusses the layering of KDE plots with histograms, detailing methods to plot both simultaneously and customize histogram parameters for density representation.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) Overview
children.summary: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) is introduced as a method to visualize the distribution of data points. The process involves summing normalized kernels at each point in the distribution to create a smooth curve that approximates a probability distribution. The final KDE curve is plotted using the syntax 'sns.kdeplot(points, bw_method=0.65)', alongside a histogram for density representation. Each line in the resulting plot represents a data point, such as a country's HIV rate, summarizing the overall distribution.

children.title: KDE Plot and Bandwidth Explanation
children.summary: The segment discusses Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) plots, specifically using a dataset referred to as 'points' and setting a bandwidth of 0.65, which is described as an arbitrary choice for visual appeal. The KDE plot illustrates the shape of the distribution, resembling a bell curve, with the density at each point calculated based on kernels placed on all data points. Additionally, a histogram is mentioned, showing the density of the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we call C born KDE plot. We say that, okay, my data set or my data frame is called points. Here I'm saying that my bandwidth is equal to 0.65. This is like really arbitrary value that I just chose in order for this plot to really look good. Which basically says that, okay, if you have a bandwidth of what, one, this is gonna be the shape of your bell curve. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: KDE and Histogram Plotting in Python
children.summary: Decreasing the bandwidth in a Kernel Density Estimate (KDE) plot affects the standard deviation and can create noticeable spikes in the visualization. The KDE plot can be layered with a histogram by using plotting functions that write to the same figure. To visualize both, the KDE can be plotted first, followed by the histogram, specifying that the histogram should display density with two bins. Alternatively, a single function call can be used to plot both the histogram and the KDE by setting the KDE parameter to true. This approach simplifies the plotting process while still allowing for customization of the histogram's bins and density representation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And if you, Decrease the bandwidths, then basically what you're going to have is that the bandwidths or the standard division is going to get the small larger going to have this spike here Build here, which is what's happening in this case. On top of the KDE plot, you can call all of these plotting functions on top of each other. Under the hood, there is a figure that each plot function just writes on it. it. So I first plot the KDE, then on top of that, I go ahead and plot the his plot, because I want to see the histograms. So the KDE plot gives you this histogram. We'll give you all of these bars that you have, which I'm telling you that I want this stat to be density, because the KDE plot is going to give me density, and I want the number of bins to be equal to two. That's why I see two bins. That's all the control we have. There's also another way that you can plot it using only the his plot. without making two separate calls. That was for you to see that there is a KDE function that you can directly call and just to get this newscare. But if you want to get both of them together, you can call his plot. You can, I'm just going to walk you through all these arguments. Passing the data frame, saying that I want two bins in my histogram, I'm saying that I want the KDE to be plotted as well. It knows that I want KDE. So I say KDE equal to true. The default value is false. If you say kd equal to true, it'll make another call to the kd function, and it will plot that as well. leaf_chunk.audio: And if you, Decrease the bandwidths, then basically what you're going to have is that the bandwidths or the standard division is going to get the small larger going to have this spike here Build here, which is what's happening in this case. On top of the KDE plot, you can call all of these plotting functions on top of each other. Under the hood, there is a figure that each plot function just writes on it. it. So I first plot the KDE, then on top of that, I go ahead and plot the his plot, because I want to see the histograms. So the KDE plot gives you this histogram. We'll give you all of these bars that you have, which I'm telling you that I want this stat to be density, because the KDE plot is going to give me density, and I want the number of bins to be equal to two. That's why I see two bins. That's all the control we have. There's also another way that you can plot it using only the his plot. without making two separate calls. That was for you to see that there is a KDE function that you can directly call and just to get this newscare. But if you want to get both of them together, you can call his plot. You can, I'm just going to walk you through all these arguments. Passing the data frame, saying that I want two bins in my histogram, I'm saying that I want the KDE to be plotted as well. It knows that I want KDE. So I say KDE equal to true. The default value is false. If you say kd equal to true, it'll make another call to the kd function, and it will plot that as well.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) Explanation
children.summary: Density is plotted using Kernel Density Estimation (KDE), with arguments passed in a dictionary format for the kde function. The example provided uses sns.histplot to summarize the distribution, showcasing how density is calculated based on kernels placed on all data points, such as one country's HIV rate.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Displot Function
children.summary: The segment discusses Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) and its role in visualizing data distributions. An example using the seaborn library illustrates how to plot a histogram with KDE to summarize a dataset, highlighting parameters like bandwidth (set to 0.65) and tail extensions. Additionally, the 'displot' function is introduced as a versatile tool for visualizing distributions, specifically demonstrating its application in plotting the distribution of gross national income per capita.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Displot Usage
children.summary: The discussion covers the concept of Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) and its application in visualizing data distributions. An example is provided using the seaborn library, where a histogram is plotted with KDE to summarize a dataset. The parameters for the KDE, such as bandwidth (set to 0.65) and the extension of tails, are explained. The segment also introduces the 'displot' function as a wrapper for various plotting functions to visualize distributions, specifically demonstrating its use for plotting the distribution of gross national income per capita.

Hot to be cool to tree is kind of a nice thing, which will tell you that, okay, go ahead and extend the tails of this distribution, tree standard deviation from the minimum and from the maximum. So that's why you're seeing all of these hearts right here. This is because we're not cutting it at the exact location of the pins, but we're just letting it to smoothly drop onto zero and tree standard deviation. from the maximum or from the minimum of these parts. children.title: Using displot for Distribution Visualization and KDE Overview
children.summary: The displot function in seaborn is introduced as a versatile tool for plotting distributions, including histograms, KDE, and ECDF. An example with the wb dataset illustrates how to plot gross national income (GNI) data using sns.displot with various kinds. The segment explains Kernel Density Estimation (KDE), detailing its three-step process involving kernel functions centered on data points, the importance of bandwidth, and the general formula for KDE. The Gaussian kernel is highlighted as a common choice. Additionally, the segment discusses the characteristics of valid kernel functions, including non-negativity and integration to 1, and how the alpha parameter controls bandwidth, influencing the probability drop-off.

children.title: Using displot for Histogram, KDE, and ECDF
children.summary: The displot function in seaborn is introduced as a versatile tool for plotting distributions, serving as a wrapper for histplot, kdeplot, and ecdfplot. An example using the wb dataset demonstrates how to plot gross national income (GNI) data with the syntax sns.displot(data=wb, x='gni', kind='hist', stat='density'), which creates a histogram with density on the y-axis. It is noted that using kind='kde' defaults to density, streamlining the creation of density plots. Additionally, the empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) plot is explained, showing the percentage of data points below each individual data point as an alternative to traditional plots. The segment emphasizes the effective visualization of GNI per capita using various kinds such as 'hist', 'kde', and 'ecdf'.

children.title: Using displot for Histogram and KDE
children.summary: The displot function in seaborn is introduced as a wrapper for histplot, kdeplot, and ecdfplot, facilitating the plotting of distributions. An example is provided using the wb dataset to plot gross national income (GNI) data. The syntax sns.displot(data=wb, x='gni', kind='hist', stat='density') is explained, indicating that it creates a histogram where the y-axis represents density. Additionally, when using kind='kde', the density is the default stat, simplifying the process of creating density plots.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There's also another function that can plot histograms, that can also give us the KDE functions. And that's called this plot. This plot is what it gets is it will get something that's called kind. Which is similar to the native panda plotting. Remember that I would do plot kind equal to bar. This is kind of the same thing. This plot is a wrapper on top of the KDE plot his plots on and so forth so that you don't need to remember which one is which. So you just directly call this plot, meaning that I want to plot the distribution. But what do I want to plot about the distribution? I want to plot using the WB data set. I want to plot GNI data. I want to call that the type to be or the kind to be histogram. And I want the stat that you're showing on the y-axis to be density. It will create histogram for you. You could say that, okay, I'm doing this plot, but here I'm saying that the kind is equal to KDE. You don't need to specify that this stat is density because by default this stat is density because it is a density function at the end when you're creating the KDE plot. leaf_chunk.audio: There's also another function that can plot histograms, that can also give us the KDE functions. And that's called this plot. This plot is what it gets is it will get something that's called kind. Which is similar to the native panda plotting. Remember that I would do plot kind equal to bar. This is kind of the same thing. This plot is a wrapper on top of the KDE plot his plots on and so forth so that you don't need to remember which one is which. So you just directly call this plot, meaning that I want to plot the distribution. But what do I want to plot about the distribution? I want to plot using the WB data set. I want to plot GNI data. I want to call that the type to be or the kind to be histogram. And I want the stat that you're showing on the y-axis to be density. It will create histogram for you. You could say that, okay, I'm doing this plot, but here I'm saying that the kind is equal to KDE. You don't need to specify that this stat is density because by default this stat is density because it is a density function at the end when you're creating the KDE plot.

children.title: ECDF Plot and sns.displot Usage
children.summary: The empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF) plot illustrates the percentage of data points below each individual data point, serving as an alternative to traditional distribution plots. The 'sns.displot' function acts as a wrapper for plotting distributions using histograms, KDE plots, and ECDF plots. Examples include plotting the distribution of gross national income (GNI) per capita using different kinds such as 'hist', 'kde', and 'ecdf', showcasing how to visualize data effectively.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There's also this last thing that is called ECDF plot, which is empirical cumulative distribution function, which basically tells you like the cumulative distribution function that you want to create that like what is the percentage of data points that are below each individual data point. So you can go crazy instead of having the distribution plot, you can have the ECDF plot as well. So on that aside, let's go back to the steps that we took in order to create the KDE plot.
