children.title: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) Process and Function
children.summary: The segment provides an overview of Kernel Density Estimation (KDE), detailing the three steps involved in the process. It explains how a kernel function is centered on each sample data point along the x-axis, with the bandwidth affecting its shape. The function alpha(x, x_i) represents the kernel centered at each data point x_i. A general formula for KDE is introduced, emphasizing that each kernel has an area of 1. The discussion includes the scaling of data points by a factor of 1/n and the summation over all data points, applying a kernel to each. The Gaussian kernel is highlighted as a common example, characterized by its bell curve shape, and the choice of kernel function will be elaborated on later.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) Overview
children.summary: The segment explains the process of Kernel Density Estimation (KDE), emphasizing the three steps involved. It describes how a kernel function is centered on each sample data point on the x-axis, where the kernel function, denoted as alpha, has a bandwidth that affects its shape. The function alpha(x, x_i) represents the kernel centered at each data point x_i, allowing for different x values. The segment also introduces a general formula for KDE, highlighting that each kernel has an area of 1 and mentions that the choice of kernel function will be discussed in detail later.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Remember that we had three different steps. So I'm going to put that in one equation and show you what those three steps were. It sounds a little bit complicated, but it is not. So the first step for us was to put the kernel on top of each one of those locations, right? When I say putting a kernel on top of each one of those locations, that means that we would say that I want a function that is on the x axis, it can take different x values. But it is centered around x i, it is centered around each one of the sample data points that you have because we're putting each one of these kernels on top of each individual data point. Okay. Alpha x and x i basically is the kernel function alpha is the bandwidth of that kernel function x is a value that it can take it can be any value on the x axis x i is various the center of that curve so you're basically thinking about putting the curve on each one of the data points. children.title: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) Overview
children.summary: Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) involves scaling down data points by a factor of 1/n and summing over all data points, applying a kernel to each individual point. The resulting function, parametrized by alpha, varies based on the choice of alpha. Different types of kernels can be used, with the Gaussian kernel being a notable example, characterized by its bell curve shape centered around each data point. Each kernel has an area of 1, and the mathematical details of these functions will be discussed further.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Then what we did was effectively a scaling it down. Remember, this was a scaling it down by a factor of 1 over n if you have n data points. And then the other factor here is that you're going to sum up over all of the possible data points that you have. If you have n different data points, you need to put a kernel on top of each individual one of them. That's where your i comes from. That's why we're, that's where your x i comes from. So put a kernel on top of each one of them, scale it down, sum it up, and at the end you have a function. And what is this function? This is a function of x. X can take any values. And then this function is parametrized by alpha. depending on the choice of alpha, then the type of the care and elensity estimate, the KDE function that you're going to get at the end is going to be different. So quickly going over different types of kernels. We have the Gaussian kernel. The equation for a Gaussian kernel is not that important. You might have seen it before. The Gaussian kernel is that bell curve shape kind of function that you have that is centered around X i. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: Then what we did was effectively a scaling it down. Remember, this was a scaling it down by a factor of 1 over n if you have n data points. And then the other factor here is that you're going to sum up over all of the possible data points that you have. If you have n different data points, you need to put a kernel on top of each individual one of them. That's where your i comes from. That's why we're, that's where your x i comes from. So put a kernel on top of each one of them, scale it down, sum it up, and at the end you have a function. And what is this function? This is a function of x. X can take any values. And then this function is parametrized by alpha. depending on the choice of alpha, then the type of the care and elensity estimate, the KDE function that you're going to get at the end is going to be different. children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Bandwidth Control
children.summary: The maximum value of a probability density function occurs at x equals x_i, where the function integrates to 1. A valid kernel function, such as the Gaussian kernel, must be non-negative and integrate to 1. The parameter alpha controls the bandwidth of the kernel, affecting the probability drop-off from x_i; larger alpha values create sharper peaks. In Kernel Density Estimation (KDE), increasing alpha enhances curve smoothness, with alpha values of one and two reducing noise and further smoothing, respectively. The choice of alpha impacts the density of the distribution, with higher values potentially obscuring details like multimodality. The discussion also includes the boxcar kernel, which is less common but maintains an area under the curve of one.

children.title: Maximum Value of Probability Density Function
children.summary: The maximum value of the probability density curve occurs at the point where x equals x_i, resulting in the numerator being zero and e to the power of zero equaling one. As x deviates from x_i, the probability decreases. The total area under the curve, calculated by summing this function over all possible values, equals 1. A kernel, defined as a valid density function, must be non-negative for all inputs and integrate to 1. The Gaussian kernel is highlighted as the most common example.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So you would see that the maximum value that this curve can take is at the point that x is equal to x i. So that that numerator is equal to zero. So that e to the power of zero is equal to one. That's where we have the maximum value. And then as x is going to deviate from x i on both sides, then the probability is going to drop. And if you calculate this summation for of this function over all possible values, you will see that the area under the curve is equal to 1. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

children.title: Kernel Functions and Bandwidth Control
children.summary: A kernel function is defined as a valid density function that is non-negative for all inputs and integrates to 1. The Gaussian kernel is a common example. The parameter alpha controls the bandwidth of the kernel, influencing how sharply the probability drops as one moves away from a specific data point (xi). A larger alpha results in a sharper drop-off, creating a more centered peak. Different bandwidths can lead to varying distributions; for instance, a small alpha value (0.1) produces a spiky distribution when averaging data points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And obviously, you know that this function is always positive, so it is a probability function that we can work with. So these are some details that center the wrong x i and Alpha controls the bandwidth. So here you're seeing that when I say that the kernel function is parametrized by alpha and I keep using alpha or bandwidth, it's because alpha appears here and alpha appears here, and alpha controls that bandwidth. So it will basically tell you that as you're moving away, if you're looking at that exponent thing at the top here, it will tell you that as you're moving away from Xi, how sharp should you drop the probability. Right, when alpha is a large value, you should drop the probability sharply because the denominator is large. Right, so that's how you can make sense of why alpha controls the bandwidth and because you're dropping sharply, it's going to be making a very kind of makes a very kind of centered peak. The effect of band myths we can see it here. We could say that, okay, if I have a distribution and my distribution, if I look at it from the histogram perspective is like this, and I use different band myths, you're seeing that with alpha equal to point point 1, it is going to be very spiky, because I'm putting a spiky stuff on top of the data points and I'm averaging all of them. leaf_chunk.audio: And obviously, you know that this function is always positive, so it is a probability function that we can work with. So these are some details that center the wrong x i and Alpha controls the bandwidth. So here you're seeing that when I say that the kernel function is parametrized by alpha and I keep using alpha or bandwidth, it's because alpha appears here and alpha appears here, and alpha controls that bandwidth. So it will basically tell you that as you're moving away, if you're looking at that exponent thing at the top here, it will tell you that as you're moving away from Xi, how sharp should you drop the probability. Right, when alpha is a large value, you should drop the probability sharply because the denominator is large. Right, so that's how you can make sense of why alpha controls the bandwidth and because you're dropping sharply, it's going to be making a very kind of makes a very kind of centered peak. The effect of band myths we can see it here. We could say that, okay, if I have a distribution and my distribution, if I look at it from the histogram perspective is like this, and I use different band myths, you're seeing that with alpha equal to point point 1, it is going to be very spiky, because I'm putting a spiky stuff on top of the data points and I'm averaging all of them.

children.title: Effect of Alpha on Kernel Density Estimation
children.summary: Increasing the alpha parameter in Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) affects the smoothness of the resulting curve. An alpha of one reduces some noise, while an alpha of two further smooths the curve. The choice of alpha influences the density of the 'fabric' over the distribution, with higher values creating a thicker fabric that simplifies the KDE but may obscure important distributional details, such as multimodality.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So those spikes are not going to merge together and form a nice kind of curvey shape. If I increase alpha to equal to one, I will start losing some of those noise that I saw in the previous case, but there is still some noise exists in my data. If I increase alpha to be equal to two, you're seeing that my curve is getting smoother and it's smoother in this case as well. So the choice of alpha is really your choice, but you just know that based on the alpha, you're changing, remember that fabric thing, you're changing the density of that fabric. So if your alpha is going to be a very high alpha, you're putting a very thick fabric on top of your distribution. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: So those spikes are not going to merge together and form a nice kind of curvey shape. If I increase alpha to equal to one, I will start losing some of those noise that I saw in the previous case, but there is still some noise exists in my data. If I increase alpha to be equal to two, you're seeing that my curve is getting smoother and it's smoother in this case as well. So the choice of alpha is really your choice, but you just know that based on the alpha, you're changing, remember that fabric thing, you're changing the density of that fabric. So if your alpha is going to be a very high alpha, you're putting a very thick fabric on top of your distribution.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Bandwidth Effects
children.summary: The discussion focuses on kernel density estimation (KDE) and the impact of different kernels, particularly the Gaussian and boxcar kernels. The boxcar kernel, while not commonly used in practice, is described as being parametrized by alpha and centered on specific data points, maintaining an area under the curve equal to one. The significance of bandwidth in KDE is emphasized, comparing it to the width of bins in a histogram; as bandwidth increases, the KDE becomes smoother, which simplifies interpretation but may obscure important distributional details, such as multimodality.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So only the single peak is going to be captured. If you keep like lightening or choosing a fabric that's a little bit lighter, you will get a sense of all of those smaller peaks or smaller trends. So that's kind of a choice when you're making in order to make sure that do you understand enough about your distribution, do you understand what is the mood of your distribution and the skewness of the distribution and so on and so forth. There are other types of kernels such as box car kernel. It's not going to be used in practice, but you would basically see that it's still parametrized by alpha. It still has the center on x i's and the shape is going to be something like this. Area under the box is always equal to one. It's center that x i, for example, in this case, it's equal to four. for and then it takes in a value for the height or for the density so that the area under the curve is equal to one. And if we apply boxcar instead of the Gaussian kernel for the kernel density estimation, this is the kernel density estimation that we're going to see with a different kernel. But in practice most of the time we'll use Gaussian kernel. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: So only the single peak is going to be captured. If you keep like lightening or choosing a fabric that's a little bit lighter, you will get a sense of all of those smaller peaks or smaller trends. So that's kind of a choice when you're making in order to make sure that do you understand enough about your distribution, do you understand what is the mood of your distribution and the skewness of the distribution and so on and so forth. There are other types of kernels such as box car kernel. It's not going to be used in practice, but you would basically see that it's still parametrized by alpha. It still has the center on x i's and the shape is going to be something like this. Area under the box is always equal to one. It's center that x i, for example, in this case, it's equal to four. for and then it takes in a value for the height or for the density so that the area under the curve is equal to one. And if we apply boxcar instead of the Gaussian kernel for the kernel density estimation, this is the kernel density estimation that we're going to see with a different kernel. But in practice most of the time we'll use Gaussian kernel.

children.title: Kernel Density Estimation and Practice Poll
children.summary: Kernel density estimation (KDE) plots can take various shapes depending on the kernel function used, but key properties include that the area under the curve must equal one and the function must remain positive across all data points to qualify as a probability function. The boxcar kernel was introduced as a theoretical alternative, assigning uniform density within a specified window of observations, resembling a histogram but not practically useful in the course context. A practice poll was conducted to identify valid kernel density plots, reinforcing understanding of the concepts discussed.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So we'll always see all of those like curvy shapes or nice curves that you're seeing that you are in the demo today. So this is the last thing. Just as a practice if you haven't had a chance to respond to any of this slide Let's go ahead and tell us which one of these are a carnal density estimate plot or a KDE plot. You can see that depending on the on the kernel function it can take different shapes but what's important is that the area under the care should be equal to one the function should always be positive for all of the data points for it to be a probability function so those are the important pieces And I believe this is the last slide of the day. If you are done... a great weekend. I will see you next week on Tuesday. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

leaf_chunk.audio: So we'll always see all of those like curvy shapes or nice curves that you're seeing that you are in the demo today. So this is the last thing. Just as a practice if you haven't had a chance to respond to any of this slide Let's go ahead and tell us which one of these are a carnal density estimate plot or a KDE plot. You can see that depending on the on the kernel function it can take different shapes but what's important is that the area under the care should be equal to one the function should always be positive for all of the data points for it to be a probability function so those are the important pieces And I believe this is the last slide of the day. If you are done... a great weekend. I will see you next week on Tuesday.
